17  Comparing Drugs Under Realistic Adherence: An LMTP Case Study

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
19 Overview
Estimated reading time: 55 minutes
Chapter 3.3 introduced a distribution-shift intervention using quantile mapping to compare two antiretroviral drugs under a common adherence distribution. That approach is one principled way to operationalize the question “what if Drug B patients adhered like Drug A patients?”, but it is not the only way. Different shift functions encode different assumptions about what “adhering like Drug A” means, and they answer subtly different causal questions.
This chapter implements four approaches on the same simulated data:
Each approach defines a different modified treatment policy for lmtp (Dı́az et al. 2023). We run all four, compare estimates, and explain what scientific question each one answers.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
22.1 How it works
For each Drug B patient at each visit, we find where their adherence falls in the Drug B empirical CDF and map it to the corresponding quantile of the Drug A empirical CDF. A Drug B patient at the 30th percentile of Drug B adherence gets mapped to the 30th percentile of Drug A adherence. Drug A patients are left unchanged.
Formally, for Drug B patient \(i\) at visit \(t\): \[
d_1(a_{it}) = F_{A|D=A}^{-1}\big(F_{A|D=B}(a_{it})\big)
\]
where \(F_{A|D=k}\) is the marginal CDF of adherence among drug \(k\) patients.
This is a rank-preserving transformation: a patient who was relatively compliant among Drug B patients stays relatively compliant, but their absolute adherence level shifts to reflect the Drug A distribution.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
22.2 What question it answers
What would happen to Drug B patients’ outcomes if their entire adherence distribution (the shape, spread, and location) were transplanted from the Drug A population?
This captures both the higher mean and any differences in variance or skewness between the two distributions. It asks for a wholesale distributional replacement.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
22.3 Implementation

# Precompute marginal ECDFs and quantile functions by drug and time
ecdf_B_marginal <- list()
qfun_A_marginal <- list()

for (t in 1:T_max) {
  col <- paste0("A_", t)
  vals_A <- dat_wide[[col]][dat_wide$Drug == 1 & !is.na(dat_wide[[col]])]
  vals_B <- dat_wide[[col]][dat_wide$Drug == 0 & !is.na(dat_wide[[col]])]
  ecdf_B_marginal[[t]] <- ecdf(vals_B)
  # Store sorted Drug A values for quantile lookup
  qfun_A_marginal[[t]] <- sort(vals_A)
}

shift_marginal_qmap <- function(data, trt) {
  t_idx <- as.integer(gsub("A_", "", trt))
  a_obs <- data[[trt]]
  a_shifted <- a_obs

  is_b <- data$Drug == 0 & !is.na(a_obs)
  if (any(is_b)) {
    p_rank <- ecdf_B_marginal[[t_idx]](a_obs[is_b])
    p_rank <- pmax(0.01, pmin(0.99, p_rank))
    sorted_A <- qfun_A_marginal[[t_idx]]
    # Quantile function: index into sorted Drug A values
    a_shifted[is_b] <- sorted_A[pmin(length(sorted_A),
                                      ceiling(p_rank * length(sorted_A)))]
  }
  a_shifted
}


fit_qmap <- lmtp_sdr(
  data = dat_wide,
  trt = trt_vars,
  outcome = "Y",
  baseline = baseline,
  time_vary = time_vary,
  cens = cens_vars,
  shift = shift_marginal_qmap,
  mtp = TRUE,
  outcome_type = "binomial",
  learners_outcome = sl_outcome,
  learners_trt = sl_trt,
  folds = n_folds
)

contrast_qmap <- lmtp_contrast(fit_qmap, ref = fit_observed, type = "additive")
cat("Marginal quantile mapping:\n")
#> Marginal quantile mapping:
cat("  Shifted estimate:", round(fit_qmap$estimate@x, 4), "\n")
#>   Shifted estimate: 0.033
cat("  Contrast vs observed:", round(contrast_qmap$estimates$estimate, 4),
    " (95% CI:", round(contrast_qmap$estimates$conf.low, 4),
    "to", round(contrast_qmap$estimates$conf.high, 4), ")\n")
#>   Contrast vs observed: -0.1858  (95% CI: -0.2646 to -0.107 )
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
22.4 Interpretation
A negative contrast means the adherence distribution shift reduces virologic failure. The magnitude tells you how much of the Drug A advantage is explained by adherence differences. However, this approach ignores that the adherence gap may differ across patient subgroups; an older patient’s adherence gap between drugs may not be the same as a younger patient’s. Approach 2 addresses this.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
23.1 How it works
The marginal quantile map treats all Drug B patients as exchangeable within the Drug B group. But adherence depends on patient characteristics: older patients, those with lower CD4, those with higher viral load may adhere differently.
A more principled mapping conditions on covariates, so that the quantile lookup reflects each patient’s clinical profile. The idea is the same as approach 1 (find a patient’s percentile rank in the Drug B distribution and map it to the corresponding percentile in the Drug A distribution), but now both the CDF and quantile function are conditional on covariates. We estimate these using conditional quantile regression via the quantreg package, fitting separate models at a grid of quantile levels (\(\tau = 0.05, 0.10, \ldots, 0.95\)) for each drug. This avoids the normality assumption that a location-scale model would impose, which matters because adherence is bounded on [0,1] and often skewed.
For a Drug B patient with covariates \(X\) and observed adherence \(a_t\):

Evaluate the fitted Drug B conditional quantiles \(\hat{Q}_{B}(\tau \mid X)\) across the grid and find the \(\tau^*\) where \(a_t\) falls (by interpolation).
Evaluate the Drug A conditional quantile at the same level: \(a_{\text{shifted}} = \hat{Q}_A(\tau^* \mid X)\).

This is a covariate-conditional version of the CDF-inversion used in approach 1.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
23.2 What question it answers
What would happen if each Drug B patient’s adherence were set to the level they would most plausibly have achieved had they been on Drug A, given their specific clinical profile?
Two Drug B patients with the same adherence but different covariate profiles may get different shifts, because their covariate-conditional distributions under Drug A differ. A 60-year-old with low CD4 might see a larger shift than a 30-year-old with high CD4, if the Drug A advantage is larger for sicker patients.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
23.4 Interpretation
If the conditional and marginal approaches give similar results, the adherence gap between drugs is roughly constant across patient subgroups. If they diverge, the marginal approach is masking heterogeneity: the adherence shift matters more for some patients than others. The conditional approach is generally preferred when covariate-adherence relationships differ meaningfully between drugs, because it avoids shifting patients to implausible adherence levels given their clinical profile.
The quantile regression approach has two advantages over a simpler location-scale normal model. First, it makes no distributional assumption on the residuals, which is important because adherence is bounded on [0,1] and often skewed. Second, it allows the variance and shape of the conditional adherence distribution to vary with covariates, not just the mean. The cost is computational: we fit \(2 \times T \times |\tau\text{-grid}|\) quantile regressions. Quantile crossing (where predicted quantiles are not monotone) is handled by sorting, which is a standard practical fix.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
24.1 How it works
This is the simplest approach. Compute the gap between mean adherence under Drug A and Drug B at each visit, then add that gap to every Drug B patient’s adherence. Drug A patients are left unchanged.
\[
d_3(a_{it}) = \min\big(a_{it} + \delta_t, \; 1\big) \quad \text{where} \quad \delta_t = \bar{A}_t^{D=A} - \bar{A}_t^{D=B}
\]
In words: add the mean adherence gap between drugs to each Drug B patient’s observed adherence, capping at 1 because adherence above 100% is meaningless.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
24.2 What question it answers
What would happen if we could increase every Drug B patient’s adherence by a uniform amount, so that Drug B’s average adherence matched Drug A’s?
This is a cruder intervention than quantile mapping. It shifts the location of the distribution but preserves the shape (variance, skewness) of Drug B’s adherence distribution. A highly adherent Drug B patient and a poorly adherent one both get the same absolute increase, which may push some patients against the ceiling of 1. The question it answers is closer to “what if an adherence support program raised all Drug B patients’ compliance by a fixed amount?” than to “what if Drug B patients behaved exactly like Drug A patients?”
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
24.3 Implementation

# Compute the mean adherence gap at each visit
adh_gaps <- numeric(T_max)
for (t in 1:T_max) {
  col <- paste0("A_", t)
  mean_A <- mean(dat_wide[[col]][dat_wide$Drug == 1], na.rm = TRUE)
  mean_B <- mean(dat_wide[[col]][dat_wide$Drug == 0], na.rm = TRUE)
  adh_gaps[t] <- mean_A - mean_B
  cat("Visit", t, ": Drug A mean =", round(mean_A, 3),
      ", Drug B mean =", round(mean_B, 3),
      ", gap =", round(adh_gaps[t], 3), "\n")
}
#> Visit 1 : Drug A mean = 0.645 , Drug B mean = 0.412 , gap = 0.233 
#> Visit 2 : Drug A mean = 0.681 , Drug B mean = 0.423 , gap = 0.259 
#> Visit 3 : Drug A mean = 0.728 , Drug B mean = 0.449 , gap = 0.279 
#> Visit 4 : Drug A mean = 0.757 , Drug B mean = 0.473 , gap = 0.284

shift_mean_calibrated <- function(data, trt) {
  t_idx <- as.integer(gsub("A_", "", trt))
  a_obs <- data[[trt]]
  a_shifted <- a_obs

  is_b <- data$Drug == 0 & !is.na(a_obs)
  if (any(is_b)) {
    a_shifted[is_b] <- pmin(a_obs[is_b] + adh_gaps[t_idx], 1)
  }
  a_shifted
}


fit_mean <- lmtp_sdr(
  data = dat_wide,
  trt = trt_vars,
  outcome = "Y",
  baseline = baseline,
  time_vary = time_vary,
  cens = cens_vars,
  shift = shift_mean_calibrated,
  mtp = TRUE,
  outcome_type = "binomial",
  learners_outcome = sl_outcome,
  learners_trt = sl_trt,
  folds = n_folds
)

contrast_mean <- lmtp_contrast(fit_mean, ref = fit_observed, type = "additive")
cat("Mean-calibrated additive shift:\n")
#> Mean-calibrated additive shift:
cat("  Shifted estimate:", round(fit_mean$estimate@x, 4), "\n")
#>   Shifted estimate: 0.0329
cat("  Contrast vs observed:", round(contrast_mean$estimates$estimate, 4),
    " (95% CI:", round(contrast_mean$estimates$conf.low, 4),
    "to", round(contrast_mean$estimates$conf.high, 4), ")\n")
#>   Contrast vs observed: -0.1859  (95% CI: -0.2558 to -0.116 )
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
24.4 Interpretation
The mean-calibrated shift tends to produce slightly different results than the quantile map because it does not reshape the distribution. If Drug B’s adherence distribution has heavier left tail than Drug A’s (more patients with very low adherence), the additive shift pushes those patients up but does not compress the tail the way quantile mapping would. Conversely, high-adherence Drug B patients may hit the ceiling at 1, wasting some of the shift. If the estimate is similar to the quantile-mapping result, the distributional shape difference between drugs is not playing a large role.
This approach is the easiest to explain to a clinical audience: “we added X percentage points to every Drug B patient’s adherence at each visit.” It is also the most conservative, because it does not attempt to match distributional shape.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
25.1 How it works
This is the canonical LMTP modified treatment policy. Instead of calibrating the shift to match Drug A, we apply a fixed, small, uniform increase to every patient’s adherence at every visit, regardless of drug assignment:
\[
d_4(a_{it}) = \min(a_{it} + \delta, \; 1)
\]
where \(\delta\) is a prespecified amount (we use \(\delta = 0.10\), i.e., a 10 percentage-point increase). In words: add 10 percentage points to every patient’s adherence, regardless of drug assignment. This is not a cross-drug comparison at all; it is a within-population policy question.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
25.2 What question it answers
What would happen if a universal adherence support program (SMS reminders, pill organizers, peer support) increased every patient’s adherence by 10 percentage points?
This is the most directly policy-relevant framing. It does not reference Drug A’s distribution at all. Instead, it asks about the marginal benefit of a small, feasible adherence improvement across the entire cohort. Because the shift is small, it stays close to the observed data, minimizing positivity concerns. This is the intervention most naturally suited to the LMTP framework.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
25.3 Implementation

delta_fixed <- 0.10

shift_fixed <- function(data, trt) {
  a_obs <- data[[trt]]
  pmin(a_obs + delta_fixed, 1)
}


fit_fixed <- lmtp_sdr(
  data = dat_wide,
  trt = trt_vars,
  outcome = "Y",
  baseline = baseline,
  time_vary = time_vary,
  cens = cens_vars,
  shift = shift_fixed,
  mtp = TRUE,
  outcome_type = "binomial",
  learners_outcome = sl_outcome,
  learners_trt = sl_trt,
  folds = n_folds
)

contrast_fixed <- lmtp_contrast(fit_fixed, ref = fit_observed, type = "additive")
cat("Fixed additive shift (+", delta_fixed * 100, "pp):\n")
#> Fixed additive shift (+ 10 pp):
cat("  Shifted estimate:", round(fit_fixed$estimate@x, 4), "\n")
#>   Shifted estimate: 0.1883
cat("  Contrast vs observed:", round(contrast_fixed$estimates$estimate, 4),
    " (95% CI:", round(contrast_fixed$estimates$conf.low, 4),
    "to", round(contrast_fixed$estimates$conf.high, 4), ")\n")
#>   Contrast vs observed: -0.0305  (95% CI: -0.1005 to 0.0395 )
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
25.4 Interpretation
The fixed shift typically produces a smaller effect than the cross-drug approaches because the shift magnitude (\(\delta = 0.10\)) is smaller than the Drug A minus Drug B gap. But the estimand is more directly useful for program planning. If a health system can fund an intervention expected to raise adherence by about 10 percentage points, this estimate tells them the expected outcome improvement.
Note that this approach applies to all patients, not just Drug B. If you want a Drug-B-specific version, wrap the shift in a drug check (as in approaches 1–3). But the universal version is the standard LMTP estimand and has the strongest positivity properties, since the shift is small and applies uniformly.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
section: 27.1 Connecting shifts to the forgiveness curve
text:
27.1 Connecting shifts to the forgiveness curve
Each of the four shift approaches above gives a different slice of a more fundamental object: the forgiveness curve. The forgiveness curve maps a sustained adherence level to the counterfactual risk of virologic failure:
\[
r_d(a) = P\big(Y = 1 \mid \text{do}(\bar{A} = a), D = d\big)
\]
where \(d\) indexes the drug. This curve captures how a drug responds to adherence variation. A “forgiving” drug has a flat curve over a wide PDC range (failure risk stays low even when adherence drops moderately). A less forgiving drug has a steep curve where small adherence drops produce large risk increases.
Each of the four shift approaches summarizes this curve differently:
Fixed additive shift moves each patient along the x-axis of the forgiveness curve by a constant \(\delta\). The LMTP contrast integrates the slope of the curve over the observed adherence distribution, weighted by the shift magnitude. This tells you the marginal benefit of a small adherence improvement, averaged over the patient population. It is a local derivative-like quantity when \(\delta\) is small.
Mean-calibrated additive shift also moves along the x-axis, but by a drug-specific amount calibrated to close the mean adherence gap. It integrates over a larger portion of the curve but still preserves the shape of Drug B’s adherence distribution. The contrast captures how much of the outcome gap between drugs is explained by the location difference in adherence distributions.
Marginal quantile mapping transforms the entire shape of Drug B’s adherence distribution to match Drug A’s. This is not a simple translation along the x-axis; it changes the variance, skewness, and tail behavior. The contrast captures the full distributional difference in adherence, integrating the forgiveness curve under a reshaped distribution.
Conditional quantile mapping does the same distributional transformation, but conditions on patient covariates. This produces a patient-specific shift that respects heterogeneity in adherence-drug relationships.
The forgiveness curve itself can be traced directly using static L-TMLE at a grid of fixed PDC thresholds. The V4 fine-grid LTMLE analysis does this with 13 sliding bins, estimating \(r_d(a)\) at PDC levels from 0.50 to 0.95 in 3–4 percentage-point increments. This traces the curve rather than summarizing it with a single contrast.
Where the curve is well-estimated. The forgiveness curve can only be reliably estimated where there is adequate positivity (enough patients maintaining each PDC level given their covariate history). In the V4 simulation, the curve is well-estimated for PDC \(\geq 0.70\), where both drugs have substantial density. Below 0.70, positivity breaks down because few patients sustain very low adherence for extended periods (patients who disengage tend to be censored). The LMTP shift approaches have an advantage here: because they stay close to the observed distribution, they avoid the low-positivity region where static regimes fail.
The forgiveness curve is thus the unifying object that connects all the estimands in this chapter and in Chapter 3.3. The different shift approaches are different ways of summarizing the same underlying causal surface, chosen based on the scientific question and the positivity constraints of the data.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
27.2 Mediation perspective






Informal vs. formal mediation



The decomposition below provides useful intuition for separating adherence-mediated from pharmacological drug effects, but it is an informal controlled direct effect approximation. For a rigorous TMLE-based mediation analysis with formal natural direct and indirect effects (NDE/NIE), identification assumptions, and the crumble package, see the Mediation Analysis chapter.


When comparing two drugs, the total effect on virologic failure has two components:
\[
\underbrace{E[Y(D=A)] - E[Y(D=B)]}_{\text{total effect}} = \underbrace{\text{direct effect}}_{\text{pharmacology}} + \underbrace{\text{indirect effect through adherence}}_{\text{adherence-mediated}}
\]
The direct effect captures pharmacological differences between drugs (potency, resistance barrier, half-life) that affect outcomes regardless of adherence. The indirect effect captures the pathway where drug assignment affects adherence (e.g., through pill burden or side effects), which in turn affects outcomes.
The quantile-matching approach provides an informal controlled direct effect (CDE). By matching Drug B’s adherence distribution to Drug A’s, we hold the adherence pathway constant and ask what outcome difference remains.
If the total observed difference between drugs is \(\Delta_{\text{total}}\) and the difference under matched adherence is \(\Delta_{\text{matched}}\), then:

\(\Delta_{\text{matched}}\) approximates the direct (pharmacological) effect: the outcome difference when both drugs have the same adherence distribution.
\(\Delta_{\text{total}} - \Delta_{\text{matched}}\) approximates the adherence-mediated component: how much of the drug difference operates through the adherence pathway.

This decomposition is informal because a formal causal mediation analysis requires additional assumptions beyond those needed for the shift analysis:

No unmeasured mediator-outcome confounders affected by treatment. In our setting, this means no unmeasured common cause of adherence and failure that is also affected by drug assignment. Time-varying confounders like CD4 and viral load are measured, but there may be unmeasured factors (e.g., motivation, social support) that affect both adherence and outcomes.
Cross-world independence. The formal natural indirect effect requires a “cross-world” counterfactual (the outcome under Drug A with the adherence one would have had under Drug B), which involves quantities from two different interventional worlds simultaneously. This is a stronger assumption than anything required for the shift analysis alone.

Despite these caveats, the shift framework provides valuable intuition about the relative importance of adherence versus pharmacology in explaining drug differences. In the V4 talk companion results, the quantile-matched comparison shows that a substantial portion of Drug A’s observed advantage comes from its better adherence profile, while a residual direct effect remains that reflects genuine pharmacological differences in forgiveness at low adherence levels.
For a formal mediation analysis using stochastic interventions, see Diaz and Hejazi (2020), who develop identification and estimation results for natural direct and indirect effects under stochastic interventions, using the same lmtp infrastructure.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution
text:
28.1 Check your understanding


Q1. Two of the four shift approaches give very similar estimates. What does that tell you?

When the marginal quantile-mapping and mean-calibrated additive shift give similar results, it means the outcome difference between drugs is driven primarily by the location (mean) of the adherence distributions, not by differences in their shape (variance, skewness, tail behavior). The quantile mapping reshapes the entire distribution while the additive shift only translates it. If both produce similar contrasts, the distributional shape difference between drugs is not playing a large role in explaining outcomes.



Q2. When would you prefer conditional over marginal quantile mapping?

When the adherence gap between drugs varies across patient subgroups. If older patients have a larger Drug A–Drug B adherence gap than younger patients, the marginal approach applies a uniform distributional transformation that may be too aggressive for young patients and too conservative for old ones. The conditional approach uses covariate-specific quantile regressions to produce patient-specific shifts, giving each patient a shift that reflects what they specifically would have achieved under the other drug given their clinical profile.



Q3. The fixed additive shift (+10 pp) is the simplest and most conservative approach. When is it the most useful?

When the question is about a concrete policy intervention rather than a cross-drug comparison. If a health system is considering funding an adherence support program (SMS reminders, pill organizers, peer counselors) expected to raise adherence by about 10 percentage points, the fixed shift directly estimates the expected outcome improvement. It answers “what would this program achieve?” without reference to any other drug, making it the most directly actionable for policy planning. It also has the strongest positivity properties because the shift is small and uniform.







Dı́az, Iván, Nicholas Williams, Katherine L Hoffman, and Edward J Schenck. 2023. “Nonparametric Causal Effects Based on Longitudinal Modified Treatment Policies.” Journal of the American Statistical Association 118 (542): 846–56. https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.2021.1955691.
crumbs: Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
18  Four Ways to Shift an Adherence Distribution

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
20 Why This Matters
Estimated reading time: 60 minutes
Readers who have worked through the LMTP adherence case study in Chapters 3.3–3.3b will recognize the idea of decomposing drug effects into adherence-mediated and pharmacological components. That decomposition was informal: it used distribution shifts to approximate a controlled direct effect. This chapter provides the formal mediation framework that places those intuitions on rigorous causal footing, with proper identification conditions and semiparametric efficient estimation (Laan and Rose 2011).
In pharmacoepidemiology, we often want to know not just whether a drug works, but how it works. Does dapagliflozin reduce cardiovascular events because it lowers blood pressure (indirect effect through the mediator), because it improves glucose control, or through some other pathway (direct effect)?
Mediation analysis decomposes a total causal effect into the part that operates through a specified intermediate variable (the indirect effect) and the part that does not (the direct effect). Getting this decomposition right requires careful attention to the estimand, the causal model, and the assumptions that connect the two. This chapter walks through the logic and implementation of TMLE-based mediation using the crumble R package.
What you will learn:
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
21.1 The Pharmacoepidemiologic Question
SGLT2 inhibitors (like dapagliflozin) have shown surprising cardiovascular benefits in type 2 diabetes trials. A natural follow-up question:

How much of dapagliflozin’s effect on reducing MACE operates through HbA1c reduction (the glycemic pathway), and how much operates through other mechanisms (blood pressure, weight loss, direct cardiac effects)?

This matters for labeling, for understanding whether other glucose-lowering drugs should have similar benefits, and for guiding future drug development.






The Setup




Treatment (A): Dapagliflozin vs. sulfonylurea
Mediator (M): 6-month HbA1c change (glycemic improvement)
Outcome (Y): 1-year MACE (binary)
Baseline confounders (W): Age, BMI, baseline HbA1c, prior CVD, eGFR, statin use
Question: How much of the \(A \to Y\) effect goes through \(A \to M \to Y\)?
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
23.1 Step 1: Define the Estimand
Mediation decomposes a total effect into pieces. But there are several competing definitions, and the choice matters because they require different assumptions.
Natural direct and indirect effects (NDE/NIE). The most common definitions. In plain language, the NDE captures the part of the treatment effect that bypasses the mediator entirely, while the NIE captures the part that works through the mediator. Together they add up to the total effect.
\[\text{NDE} = E[Y(1, M(0))] - E[Y(0, M(0))]\] \[\text{NIE} = E[Y(1, M(1))] - E[Y(1, M(0))]\]
The NDE asks: what is the effect of treatment if we could hold the mediator at the value it would have taken under control? The NIE asks: what is the effect of shifting the mediator as treatment would, while keeping treatment on? These decompose the total effect: \(\text{TE} = \text{NDE} + \text{NIE}\).
The trouble is that \(Y(1, M(0))\) is a cross-world quantity that combines treatment from one “world” (\(A=1\)) with a mediator value from another (\(A=0\)). This requires a strong, untestable assumption (no treatment-induced mediator-outcome confounding).
The natural indirect effect answers: how much of the total effect operates through the mediator? In the dapagliflozin example, the NIE quantifies how much of the cardiovascular benefit is attributable to HbA1c reduction specifically.
Randomized interventional effects (RIE). Instead of setting \(M\) to a specific counterfactual value, we draw \(M\) from its distribution under control. Think of this as asking: “What would the outcome look like if we kept the treatment on, but randomly reassigned each patient’s mediator value from the pool of mediator values seen under the control condition?” This avoids the cross-world problem by using distributions rather than fixed individual values.
\[\text{IDE} = E[Y(1, G_0)] - E[Y(0, G_0)]\] \[\text{IIE} = E[Y(1, G_1)] - E[Y(1, G_0)]\]
where \(G_a\) denotes a random draw from \(P(M \mid A = a, W)\). These do not require cross-world quantities and are identified under weaker conditions.
Recanting twins (RT). When there is an intermediate confounder \(Z\) (affected by treatment, confounding the mediator-outcome path), even randomized interventional effects need further care. The recanting-twins approach (Robins & Richardson, 2010) handles this by decomposing the effect into path-specific components using two independent “copies” of the treatment.






Which estimand should you choose?




If you are confident there is no intermediate confounder (no \(Z\)), natural effects ("N") are the most interpretable and standard choice.
If intermediate confounding is plausible or you want weaker assumptions, randomized interventional effects ("RI") are a good default.
If you have a measured intermediate confounder \(Z\) and want to decompose the effect further, recanting twins ("RT") handles this explicitly.

For this tutorial, we will estimate all three to compare.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
section: 23.2 Step 2: Causal Model and Time Ordering
text:
23.2 Step 2: Causal Model and Time Ordering
The mediation DAG for our example:

dag_simple <- dagify(
  A ~ W, M ~ A + W, Y ~ A + M + W,
  exposure = "A", outcome = "Y",
  coords = list(
    x = c(W = 1, A = 0, M = 1, Y = 2),
    y = c(W = 1, A = 0, M = 0, Y = 0)
  )
)
ggdag(dag_simple) + theme_dag()







Figure 23.1: Simple mediation DAG: W confounds all relationships, A affects Y directly and through M





The time ordering must be: \(W\) (baseline) \(\to\) \(A\) (treatment assignment) \(\to\) \(M\) (6-month HbA1c) \(\to\) \(Y\) (1-year MACE). This ordering is not negotiable. If \(M\) is measured after \(Y\), or if \(A\) and \(M\) are measured simultaneously, the decomposition is not credible.
When we add an intermediate confounder \(Z\) (e.g., blood pressure change caused by treatment, affecting both mediator trajectory and outcome):

dag_with_z <- dagify(
  A ~ W, Z ~ A + W, M ~ A + Z + W, Y ~ A + M + Z + W,
  exposure = "A", outcome = "Y",
  coords = list(
    x = c(W = 1.5, A = 0, Z = 1, M = 2, Y = 3),
    y = c(W = 1, A = 0, Z = 0, M = 0, Y = 0)
  )
)
ggdag(dag_with_z) + theme_dag()







Figure 23.2: Mediation DAG with intermediate confounder Z (affected by A, confounds M → Y)





Now \(Z\) is post-treatment and confounds \(M \to Y\). Natural effects are not identified without the cross-world assumption. Recanting twins and randomized interventional effects can handle this.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
23.3 Step 3: Identification Assumptions
To identify mediation effects, we need the standard causal assumptions from Chapter 1.2 (consistency, exchangeability, positivity) plus additional ones specific to the mediator pathway:

Consistency: as in Chapter 1.2.
No unmeasured treatment-outcome confounding: \(Y(a, m) \perp\!\!\!\perp A \mid W\). In the dapagliflozin example, this means that, conditional on baseline covariates, treatment choice is unrelated to potential MACE outcomes. This is the standard assumption from any observational ATE analysis.
No unmeasured treatment-mediator confounding: \(M(a) \perp\!\!\!\perp A \mid W\). The mediator (HbA1c change) must be independent of treatment assignment given \(W\). If physicians choose dapagliflozin for patients they expect will respond well on HbA1c, and that expectation is based on unmeasured factors, this assumption fails.
No unmeasured mediator-outcome confounding: \(Y(a, m) \perp\!\!\!\perp M \mid A, W\) (or \(A, W, Z\) if intermediate confounders exist). This fails, for example, if patient adherence patterns independently affect both HbA1c change and cardiovascular risk, and adherence is not measured.
Positivity: For all strata of \(W\), both treatment values have positive probability, and all mediator values observed under one treatment could also occur under the other. If dapagliflozin always produces HbA1c reductions larger than any observed under sulfonylurea, the density ratios needed for estimation become extreme.

For natural effects only, add:

Cross-world independence: No confounder of \(M \to Y\) is affected by \(A\). This is the assumption that fails when an intermediate confounder \(Z\) exists. It fails, for example, if the treatment changes a patient’s blood pressure, which independently affects both HbA1c trajectory and cardiovascular risk.

For randomized interventional and recanting twins effects, assumption 6 is replaced by the weaker requirement that intermediate confounders are measured and adjusted for.






Assumption checklist (expand)





Before running a mediation analysis, verify:

Treatment precedes mediator precedes outcome in time.
Baseline confounders \(W\) are measured before treatment.
No variable in \(W\) is itself affected by treatment (otherwise it belongs in \(Z\) or \(M\)).
Confounders of \(A \to Y\), \(A \to M\), and \(M \to Y\) are identified and measured.
If using natural effects: no treatment-induced mediator-outcome confounder exists (or if one does, switch to RI/RT).
If using RI or RT effects: intermediate confounders are measured and specified in the model.
Positivity: treatment groups overlap in confounder space; mediator distributions overlap across treatment arms.
The mediator is not a proxy for treatment (if \(M\) is nearly deterministic given \(A\), the indirect effect is poorly identified).

None of these can be fully verified from data. Subject-matter knowledge is required.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
23.4 Step 4: Estimation Strategy
TMLE-based mediation estimation (as implemented in crumble) works in several stages. Throughout, the target remains: decompose the total treatment effect into the portion that operates through the mediator (the indirect effect) and the portion that does not (the direct effect).

Nuisance estimation with cross-fitting. Fit flexible models for the outcome (\(E[Y \mid A, M, W]\)), the mediator distribution (\(P(M \mid A, W)\)), and the treatment mechanism (\(P(A \mid W)\)) using an ensemble of learners. Cross-fitting (sample splitting) avoids overfitting bias.
Riesz representer estimation. The targeting step in TMLE requires a “clever covariate” that depends on the estimand. For mediation, this involves density ratios of the mediator distribution under treatment vs. control. The crumble package estimates these using a neural network (the nn_module argument) rather than requiring explicit density estimation.
Targeting (fluctuation). Update the initial outcome model to solve the efficient influence function equation. This makes the estimator doubly robust: consistent if either the outcome model or the combination of treatment/mediator models is correctly specified, under appropriate regularity conditions.
Plug-in estimation and inference. Compute the effect estimate from the targeted predictions and obtain standard errors from the influence function.







Technical detail: the mediation g-computation formulas (expand)





Under the identification assumptions, the NDE and NIE can be expressed as weighted averages over the outcome model and the mediator distribution. The NDE formula compares the expected outcome under treatment versus control, holding the mediator distribution at its control-arm values. The NIE formula holds treatment on and compares how the outcome changes when the mediator distribution shifts from its control-arm shape to its treatment-arm shape.
\[\text{NDE} = \sum_m \sum_w \big[E[Y \mid A=1, M=m, W=w] - E[Y \mid A=0, M=m, W=w]\big] \cdot P(M=m \mid A=0, W=w) \cdot P(W=w)\]
\[\text{NIE} = \sum_m \sum_w E[Y \mid A=1, M=m, W=w] \cdot \big[P(M=m \mid A=1, W=w) - P(M=m \mid A=0, W=w)\big] \cdot P(W=w)\]
These require an outcome model \(E[Y \mid A, M, W]\), a mediator model \(P(M \mid A, W)\), and marginalization over \(W\). TMLE targets these functionals to achieve double robustness.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
23.5 Step 5: Diagnostics, Sensitivity, and Reporting
After estimation, check:

Propensity score overlap: extreme treatment probabilities signal positivity problems.
Mediator distribution overlap: if the mediator distributions under treatment and control barely overlap, the density ratios will be extreme and estimates unstable.
Influence function diagnostics: large or highly variable influence function values indicate fragile estimates.
Sensitivity analysis: consider how much unmeasured confounding would be needed to explain away the result (e.g., E-values for mediation, or simulation-based sensitivity).

We demonstrate diagnostics in the worked example below.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
24.1 True Causal Effects
We compute ground-truth effects from the structural model via Monte Carlo integration.

set.seed(999)
n_mc <- 50

# Mediator and Z means under each treatment
Z_mean_A0 <- -2 * 0 + 0.3 * (bmi - 31) + 0.02 * (age - 62)
Z_mean_A1 <- -2 * 1 + 0.3 * (bmi - 31) + 0.02 * (age - 62)

compute_EY <- function(a_val, m_vals, z_vals, idx) {
  lp <- -3.5 +
    -0.25 * a_val +
     0.40 * m_vals +
     0.05 * z_vals +
     0.04 * (age[idx] - 62) +
     0.02 * (bmi[idx] - 31) +
     0.90 * cvd[idx] +
    -0.01 * (egfr[idx] - 80) +
     0.25 * statin[idx] +
     0.15 * a_val * cvd[idx]
  mean(plogis(lp))
}

true_nde_vals <- numeric(n)
true_nie_vals <- numeric(n)

for (i in 1:n) {
  # Draw Z from P(Z | A=0, W) and P(Z | A=1, W)
  z0 <- rnorm(n_mc, mean = Z_mean_A0[i], sd = 4)
  z1 <- rnorm(n_mc, mean = Z_mean_A1[i], sd = 4)

  # M means given Z draws
  M_mean_fn <- function(a, z) {
    -0.3 - 0.5 * a + 0.1 * (hba1c_bl[i] - 8.2) -
      0.005 * egfr[i] + 0.01 * (age[i] - 62) + 0.02 * z
  }

  # Draw M from P(M | A=0, Z(0), W)
  m_A0_Z0 <- rnorm(n_mc, mean = M_mean_fn(0, z0), sd = 0.6)
  # Draw M from P(M | A=1, Z(1), W)
  m_A1_Z1 <- rnorm(n_mc, mean = M_mean_fn(1, z1), sd = 0.6)

  # NDE: E[Y(1, M(0), Z(0))] - E[Y(0, M(0), Z(0))]
  # (holding mediator and Z at control values)
  EY_1_M0 <- compute_EY(1, m_A0_Z0, z0, i)
  EY_0_M0 <- compute_EY(0, m_A0_Z0, z0, i)
  EY_1_M1 <- compute_EY(1, m_A1_Z1, z1, i)

  true_nde_vals[i] <- EY_1_M0 - EY_0_M0
  true_nie_vals[i] <- EY_1_M1 - EY_1_M0
}

true_NDE <- mean(true_nde_vals)
true_NIE <- mean(true_nie_vals)
true_TE  <- true_NDE + true_NIE

cat("True Total Effect:", round(true_TE, 4), "\n")
#> True Total Effect: -0.0159
cat("True NDE (direct):", round(true_NDE, 4), "\n")
#> True NDE (direct): -0.0056
cat("True NIE (via M): ", round(true_NIE, 4), "\n")
#> True NIE (via M):  -0.0104
cat("NDE + NIE:        ", round(true_NDE + true_NIE, 4), "\n")
#> NDE + NIE:         -0.0159
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
25.1 The Baron-Kenny Trap
The classic Baron-Kenny approach fits regressions for \(Y \sim A\) and \(Y \sim A + M\), then reads off coefficients. For binary outcomes, this fails for several reasons:

total_mod <- glm(Y ~ A + age + bmi + hba1c_bl + cvd + egfr + statin,
                 family = binomial, data = dat)

full_mod  <- glm(Y ~ A + M + age + bmi + hba1c_bl + cvd + egfr + statin,
                 family = binomial, data = dat)

med_mod   <- lm(M ~ A + age + bmi + hba1c_bl + cvd + egfr + statin,
                data = dat)

cat("Total effect (A without M):", round(coef(total_mod)["A"], 3), "(log-OR)\n")
#> Total effect (A without M): -0.711 (log-OR)
cat("Direct effect (A with M):  ", round(coef(full_mod)["A"], 3), "(log-OR)\n")
#> Direct effect (A with M):   -0.527 (log-OR)
cat("Treatment on mediator:     ", round(coef(med_mod)["A"], 3), "(mean change)\n")
#> Treatment on mediator:      -0.521 (mean change)

Why this is wrong:

Non-collapsibility. The log-odds ratio changes when you add \(M\) even if there is no mediation, simply because the logistic link is non-collapsible. The “change in coefficient” is not the indirect effect.
Scale dependence. The difference-in-coefficients method requires additivity on the scale of the link function, which logistic regression does not provide for risk differences.
Post-treatment confounding. Adding \(M\) (a post-treatment variable) to the regression without proper identification opens backdoor paths through any \(Z\) that affects both \(M\) and \(Y\).
No valid inference. Standard errors for the “indirect effect” (product of coefficients) rely on normality assumptions that are wrong for binary outcomes.

These are not minor quibbles; they produce qualitatively misleading answers. A proper mediation analysis requires specifying the estimand, verifying identification, and using an estimator matched to both.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
26.1 Example 1: weight_behavior Dataset
We start with the example dataset from mma::weight_behavior, which is used in the crumble README and is known to work.

library(crumble)
data(weight_behavior, package = "mma")

# Treatment: sports participation (factor with levels 1, 2)
# Outcome: bmi (continuous)
# Mediators: exercises, overweigh
# Intermediate confounder: snack (affected by sports, confounds mediator→outcome)
# Covariates: age, sex, tvhours

# --- Natural effects (assumes no intermediate confounding) ---
fit_natural <- crumble(
  data      = weight_behavior,
  trt       = "sports",
  outcome   = "bmi",
1  covar     = c("age", "sex", "tvhours"),
2  mediators = c("exercises", "overweigh"),
3  d0 = \(data, trt) factor(rep(1, nrow(data)), levels = c("1", "2")),
  d1 = \(data, trt) factor(rep(2, nrow(data)), levels = c("1", "2")),
4  effect    = "N",
5  learners  = c("mean", "glm", "earth", "ranger"),
6  nn_module = sequential_module(),
7  control   = crumble_control(crossfit_folds = 2L, epochs = 20L)
)


1

Baseline confounders of the \(A \to Y\), \(A \to M\), and \(M \to Y\) relationships; must all be measured before treatment; do not include post-treatment variables here

2

The mediator(s) on the causal pathway from \(A\) to \(Y\); crumble models \(P(M \mid A, W)\) and uses it in the Riesz representer estimation

3

d0 and d1 are intervention functions: they take the data and treatment column name and return the counterfactual treatment value for each observation; d0 sets everyone to the reference level (treatment = “1”, i.e., control), d1 sets everyone to the active level (treatment = “2”)

4

effect = "N" requests natural direct and indirect effects (NDE/NIE); requires the cross-world independence assumption (no treatment-induced confounders of \(M \to Y\)); use "RI" or "RT" if intermediate confounders exist

5

learners: the Super Learner library used for all nuisance models (outcome \(E[Y \mid A, M, W]\), mediator \(P(M \mid A, W)\), treatment \(P(A \mid W)\)); "earth" (MARS) and "ranger" (random forest) capture nonlinear mediator-outcome relationships

6

nn_module = sequential_module(): a neural network used to estimate the Riesz representer, a density ratio of the mediator distribution under treatment vs. control; this avoids explicit density estimation which is difficult in high dimensions

7

crumble_control(crossfit_folds = 2L, epochs = 20L): controls cross-fitting (2 folds here for speed) and neural network training epochs; increase to crossfit_folds = 10L, epochs = 100L for production analyses





print(fit_natural)


# --- Randomized interventional effects ---
fit_ri <- crumble(
  data      = weight_behavior,
  trt       = "sports",
  outcome   = "bmi",
  covar     = c("age", "sex", "tvhours"),
  mediators = c("exercises", "overweigh"),
1  moc       = "snack",
  d0 = \(data, trt) factor(rep(1, nrow(data)), levels = c("1", "2")),
  d1 = \(data, trt) factor(rep(2, nrow(data)), levels = c("1", "2")),
2  effect    = "RI",
  learners  = c("mean", "glm", "earth", "ranger"),
  nn_module = sequential_module(),
  control   = crumble_control(crossfit_folds = 2L, epochs = 20L)
)


1

moc (mediator-outcome confounder): the variable “snack” is affected by treatment A and confounds the M → Y path; by specifying it here, crumble models it as an intermediate confounder and adjusts the decomposition accordingly; omitting it would bias the indirect effect

2

effect = "RI" requests randomized interventional indirect effects (IDE/IIE): instead of cross-world counterfactuals, these draw the mediator from its distribution under control \(P(M \mid A=d_0, W)\), avoiding the cross-world independence assumption while still identifying the mediated pathway





print(fit_ri)


# --- Recanting twins (handles intermediate confounding explicitly) ---
fit_rt <- crumble(
  data      = weight_behavior,
  trt       = "sports",
  outcome   = "bmi",
  covar     = c("age", "sex", "tvhours"),
  mediators = c("exercises", "overweigh"),
1  moc       = "snack",
  d0 = \(data, trt) factor(rep(1, nrow(data)), levels = c("1", "2")),
  d1 = \(data, trt) factor(rep(2, nrow(data)), levels = c("1", "2")),
2  effect    = "RT",
  learners  = c("mean", "glm", "earth", "ranger"),
  nn_module = sequential_module(),
  control   = crumble_control(crossfit_folds = 2L, epochs = 20L)
)


1

moc is required for recanting twins: the method explicitly models the intermediate confounder path \(A \to Z \to M\) and \(Z \to Y\), decomposing the total effect into components that travel through \(M\), through \(Z\), and directly

2

effect = "RT" requests the recanting twins estimand (Robins & Richardson, 2010): conceptually, it imagines two independent “copies” of the world that share the same \(W\) but have independent treatment assignments, allowing path-specific effect decomposition even in the presence of the intermediate confounder \(Z\)





print(fit_rt)
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
26.2 Example 2: Simulated Pharmacoepi Data
Now we use the simulated dataset from this chapter. We need to convert treatment to a factor so that d0 and d1 return the correct type.

library(crumble)

# Prepare data: treatment must be a factor
sim_dat <- dat |>
  mutate(A = factor(A, levels = c("0", "1")))

# Define intervention functions
d0_fn <- \(data, trt) factor(rep("0", nrow(data)), levels = c("0", "1"))
d1_fn <- \(data, trt) factor(rep("1", nrow(data)), levels = c("0", "1"))

# Covariate and mediator names
covar_names <- c("age", "bmi", "hba1c_bl", "cvd", "egfr", "statin")


26.2.1 Natural effects (no intermediate confounder adjustment)

fit_sim_N <- crumble(
  data      = sim_dat,
  trt       = "A",
  outcome   = "Y",
  covar     = covar_names,
  mediators = "M",
1  d0        = d0_fn,
  d1        = d1_fn,
2  effect    = "N",
  learners  = c("mean", "glm", "earth", "ranger"),
  nn_module = sequential_module(),
  control   = crumble_control(crossfit_folds = 2L, epochs = 5L)
)


1

d0_fn and d1_fn were defined above as lambda functions that set treatment to "0" (control) or "1" (treated) for all observations; treatment must be a factor for crumble; the levels define what d0 and d1 can return

2

effect = "N" fits natural effects, valid here only if the DGP’s intermediate confounder \(Z\) can be ignored or is absent; since our DGP includes \(Z\), this estimate will be biased relative to the true NDE/NIE (compare to fit_sim_RT)





print(fit_sim_N)



26.2.2 Recanting twins (with intermediate confounder Z)
Because our DGP includes \(Z\) (blood pressure change) as a treatment-induced confounder of \(M \to Y\), recanting twins is the more appropriate estimand.

fit_sim_RT <- crumble(
  data      = sim_dat,
  trt       = "A",
  outcome   = "Y",
  covar     = covar_names,
  mediators = "M",
1  moc       = "Z",
  d0        = d0_fn,
  d1        = d1_fn,
2  effect    = "RT",
  learners  = c("mean", "glm", "earth", "ranger"),
  nn_module = sequential_module(),
  control   = crumble_control(crossfit_folds = 2L, epochs = 5L)
)


1

moc = "Z": specifies the measured intermediate confounder (blood pressure change in the pharmacoepi framing), the variable affected by treatment \(A\) that confounds the \(M \to Y\) path; crumble now models \(P(Z \mid A, W)\) and uses it in the recanting-twins identification formula

2

effect = "RT" (recanting twins) is the appropriate estimand when moc is present: it correctly handles the \(A \to Z \to M\) and \(Z \to Y\) paths, producing valid path-specific effect decompositions that the natural effects estimator cannot identify when \(Z\) exists





print(fit_sim_RT)



26.2.3 Randomized interventional effects (with Z)

fit_sim_RI <- crumble(
  data      = sim_dat,
  trt       = "A",
  outcome   = "Y",
  covar     = covar_names,
  mediators = "M",
  moc       = "Z",
  d0        = d0_fn,
  d1        = d1_fn,
1  effect    = "RI",
  learners  = c("mean", "glm", "earth", "ranger"),
  nn_module = sequential_module(),
  control   = crumble_control(crossfit_folds = 2L, epochs = 5L)
)


1

effect = "RI" (randomized interventional effects) with moc specified: instead of the recanting-twins cross-world structure, the indirect effect is defined by randomly drawing \(M\) from \(P(M \mid A=d_0, Z, W)\); this is a stochastic intervention on the mediator that avoids both the cross-world assumption and the need to model the full \(Z\) pathway explicitly





print(fit_sim_RI)







Faster vs. more accurate settings



The settings above (crossfit_folds = 2L, epochs = 5L) are deliberately fast for tutorials. For real analyses, increase to crossfit_folds = 10L, epochs = 100L (the defaults) and consider adding more flexible learners (e.g., "xgboost", "glmnet").
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
section: 27.1 What do the effects mean in context?
text:
27.1 What do the effects mean in context?
In the dapagliflozin example:

The direct effect captures the cardiovascular benefit of dapagliflozin that does not operate through HbA1c reduction. This includes blood pressure lowering, weight loss, ketone body effects, and direct cardiac remodeling. A large direct effect suggests SGLT2 inhibitors have a unique cardioprotective mechanism beyond glucose lowering.
The indirect effect (via M) captures how much of the cardiovascular benefit is attributable to the drug’s effect on HbA1c. If this is large, other glucose-lowering drugs might produce similar cardiovascular benefits.
The total effect is the sum (for additive decompositions). It should be close to what you would estimate from a standard TMLE or IPTW analysis of \(A\) on \(Y\).

The choice of effect definition matters for interpretation. Natural effects answer “what if we could intervene on the mediator to hold it at its control value?” This is a hypothetical intervention that may not be physically realizable.
Randomized interventional effects answer a different question: “what if we randomly assigned the mediator value from its distribution under control?” This stochastic intervention is closer to policy-relevant questions. Recanting twins decompose the total effect into path-specific components that remain identifiable even with intermediate confounding.
None of these claims establish “mechanism” in a biological sense without strong assumptions. The decomposition tells you how much of the effect flows through the measured mediator given the causal model and identification assumptions. Unmeasured mediator-outcome confounders, measurement error in \(M\), or misspecification of the time ordering can all distort the decomposition.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
27.2 Caution: proportion mediated
The “proportion mediated” (NIE / TE) is commonly reported but has several problems:

It is undefined when the total effect is zero or near zero.
It can be greater than 1 or negative when the direct and indirect effects have opposite signs.
It is highly variable because it is a ratio of two estimated quantities, each with uncertainty.
It is scale-dependent: the proportion mediated on the risk-difference scale differs from that on the odds-ratio scale.

Report the direct and indirect effects on the absolute scale (risk difference) with confidence intervals. If you report proportion mediated, note these caveats.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
28.1 Propensity Score Overlap
Before interpreting the indirect effect, verify that the estimation is operating in a regime where the nuisance models are well-behaved. The indirect effect answers: how much of the total treatment effect operates through the mediator? That answer is only trustworthy if the following diagnostics look reasonable.

ps <- predict(
  glm(A ~ age + bmi + hba1c_bl + cvd + egfr + statin,
      family = binomial, data = dat),
  type = "response"
)

ggplot(dat, aes(x = ps, fill = factor(A, labels = c("Sulfonylurea", "Dapagliflozin")))) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45) +
  labs(x = "P(A=1 | W)", fill = "Treatment",
       title = "Propensity Score Overlap") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#4477AA", "#EE6677"))
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
28.2 Mediator Distribution by Treatment
The indirect effect depends on treatment actually shifting the mediator distribution. If the distributions overlap completely, there is no indirect effect to detect.

ggplot(dat, aes(x = M, fill = factor(A, labels = c("Sulfonylurea", "Dapagliflozin")))) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45) +
  labs(x = "6-month HbA1c change (M)", fill = "Treatment",
       title = "Mediator Distribution by Treatment Group") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#4477AA", "#EE6677"))
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
28.3 Mediator Density Ratio
For TMLE-based mediation, the density ratio \(f(M \mid A=0, W) / f(M \mid A=1, W)\) is a key intermediate quantity. Extreme values indicate instability.

m_mod <- lm(M ~ A + age + bmi + hba1c_bl + egfr + Z, data = dat)
sigma_m <- sigma(m_mod)

M_mean_a0 <- predict(m_mod, newdata = dat |> mutate(A = 0))
M_mean_a1 <- predict(m_mod, newdata = dat |> mutate(A = 1))

density_ratio <- dnorm(dat$M, M_mean_a0, sigma_m) /
  pmax(dnorm(dat$M, M_mean_a1, sigma_m), 1e-10)

ggplot(tibble(r = density_ratio), aes(x = r)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#CC6677", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "f(M|A=0,W) / f(M|A=1,W)", y = "Count",
       title = "Mediator Density Ratio") +
  theme_minimal()








cat("Density ratio summary:\n")
#> Density ratio summary:
summary(density_ratio)
#>     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
#>  0.03958  0.59909  1.08072  1.55780  1.96807 18.87838
cat("Proportion > 3:", mean(density_ratio > 3), "\n")
#> Proportion > 3: 0.117
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
28.4 Mediator-Outcome Relationship

ggplot(dat, aes(x = M, y = Y)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, color = "#228833") +
  facet_wrap(~factor(A, labels = c("Sulfonylurea", "Dapagliflozin"))) +
  labs(x = "6-month HbA1c change (M)", y = "P(MACE)",
       title = "Mediator-Outcome Relationship by Treatment") +
  theme_minimal()

title: 19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis
text:
31.2 What comes next
Mediation analysis asks how a treatment works. The final chapter asks a complementary question for safety studies: what does your analysis actually estimate when patients switch treatments, discontinue, or experience competing events? We close with the ICH E9(R1) estimand framework for time-to-event data.






Laan, Mark J van der, and Sherri Rose. 2011. “Targeted Learning: Causal Inference for Observational and Experimental Data.” Springer.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
19  TMLE for Mediation Analysis

title: 20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning
text: 20.1 Time-to-Event Estimands
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning

title: 20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning
text:
20.1.1 Risk at Time t
The most interpretable causal estimand in survival analysis is the counterfactual risk (cumulative incidence) at a pre-specified time point t:
\[\Psi = P(T^{a=1} \leq t) - P(T^{a=0} \leq t)\]
This is a marginal risk difference: the difference in the probability of the event by time t under treatment vs. control, averaged over the target population. It has a clear causal interpretation under standard identifiability assumptions.


20.1.2 Restricted Mean Survival Time (RMST)
The RMST up to time \(\tau\) is the area under the survival curve from 0 to \(\tau\):
\[\text{RMST}(\tau) = \int_0^\tau S(t) \, dt\]
The causal contrast is the difference in RMST between treatment arms. This estimand summarizes the entire survival curve up to \(\tau\) without requiring proportional hazards, making it robust and interpretable.


20.1.3 The Hazard Ratio Problem






Hazard Ratios Are Not Straightforward Causal Parameters



The hazard ratio has two fundamental problems as a causal quantity:

Non-collapsibility: Even in the absence of confounding, the conditional hazard ratio (within strata) does not equal the marginal hazard ratio. This means that a perfectly adjusted hazard ratio from a Cox model estimates a different quantity than the marginal hazard ratio in the population—even when adjustment is correct.
Built-in selection bias: The hazard at time t is defined only among those who have survived to t. Under treatment, the survivors at time t are a selected subset of the original population. Even in a randomized trial, comparing hazards at later time points compares groups that differ in composition because the treatment has already differentially removed individuals from the risk set.

For causal inference, prefer risk differences, risk ratios at fixed time points, or RMST differences. The target trial framework (Hernán and Robins 2016) emphasizes that the causal question should drive the estimand, and hazard ratios rarely correspond to a well-defined causal contrast.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning

title: 20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning
section: 20.2 ICH E9(R1) Intercurrent Events and Censoring Strategies
text:
20.2 ICH E9(R1) Intercurrent Events and Censoring Strategies
The ICH E9(R1) addendum defines five strategies for handling intercurrent events (events that affect interpretation of the treatment effect). Each maps to a specific censoring or outcome-handling approach in TMLE.



Table 20.1: ICH E9(R1) strategies mapped to TMLE approaches










ICH E9(R1) Strategy
Description
TMLE Implementation




Treatment policy
Outcome observed regardless of intercurrent event
No censoring at intercurrent event; analyze all observed outcomes


Composite
Intercurrent event is part of the outcome
Redefine outcome to include the intercurrent event (e.g., death or discontinuation)


Hypothetical
Outcome in a world where the intercurrent event could not occur
Censor at intercurrent event and use IPCW to adjust for informative censoring


Principal stratum
Effect among those who would not experience the intercurrent event under either treatment
Requires principal stratification; typically not handled by standard TMLE


While on treatment
Outcome while on assigned treatment
Censor at treatment discontinuation; use IPCW for informative censoring






The hypothetical and while-on-treatment strategies both require IPCW. The key difference is what triggers censoring: under the hypothetical strategy, a biological event (e.g., a competing risk) triggers censoring, while under the while-on-treatment strategy, treatment discontinuation triggers censoring.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning

title: 20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning
text:
20.3 Informative Censoring and IPCW

20.3.1 The Problem
Standard Kaplan-Meier and Cox regression assume that censoring is non-informative—that censored individuals have the same future risk as those who remain under observation. When censoring is related to prognosis (e.g., sicker patients are more likely to switch treatments or drop out), this assumption is violated and naive estimates are biased.


20.3.2 Inverse Probability of Censoring Weights
IPCW corrects for informative censoring by upweighting individuals who remain uncensored but are similar to those who were censored. At each time point \(t_k\), the censoring weight for an individual still under observation is:
\[W_C(t_k) = \prod_{j=1}^{k} \frac{1}{P(C > t_j \mid C > t_{j-1}, \bar{A}_{j-1}, \bar{L}_{j-1})}\]


20.3.3 Cumulative Weight Instability






The Cumulative Product Problem



Because IPCW weights are a product over time points, instability compounds. Even if the probability of remaining uncensored at each time point is 0.95, after 20 time points the weight becomes \(1/0.95^{20} \approx 2.65\). If some individuals have per-period censoring probabilities of 0.80, their weights explode: \(1/0.80^{20} \approx 87\).
Practical consequences:

Positivity violations accumulate: At later time points, fewer covariate strata have uncensored individuals, violating positivity
Effective sample size shrinks: Even with a large nominal sample, the effective sample size can become very small at later time points
Variance inflation: A few highly weighted individuals dominate the estimate

Remedies: Truncate weights (with sensitivity analysis), restrict the analysis to a time horizon where weights remain stable, or use TMLE which can stabilize estimation through its targeting step.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning

title: 20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning
text:
20.4 Competing Risks
A competing risk is an event that prevents the outcome of interest from occurring. For example, when studying time to cardiovascular death, non-cardiovascular death is a competing event.

20.4.1 Two Approaches
Cause-specific approach: Treat competing events as censoring. This estimates the cause-specific hazard, which answers: “Among those currently alive, what is the instantaneous rate of the event of interest?” However, the resulting survival curve does not have a probability interpretation when competing risks exist.
Fine-Gray (subdistribution) approach: Keep individuals in the risk set after experiencing a competing event. This directly models the cumulative incidence function (CIF) and answers: “What is the probability of the event of interest by time t, accounting for the fact that competing events prevent it?”


20.4.2 TMLE for Competing Risks
TMLE can handle competing risks in two ways:

Competing event as censoring: Apply IPCW for the competing event, treating it like any other form of informative censoring. This targets the cause-specific risk in a hypothetical world without the competing event.
Composite outcome: Combine the event of interest and the competing event into a single outcome. This targets the risk of “any event” by time t.
Direct CIF estimation: Use a TMLE that directly targets the cumulative incidence function. This is the most principled approach and corresponds to the subdistribution quantity.

The choice depends on the scientific question:

“What would the risk be if we could eliminate the competing event?” \(\rightarrow\) Cause-specific with IPCW
“What is the actual probability of the event in the real world where competing events exist?” \(\rightarrow\) CIF approach

title: 20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning
section: 20.5 Worked Example: Survival TMLE with Competing Risks
text:
20.5 Worked Example: Survival TMLE with Competing Risks

20.5.1 Data Simulation
We simulate a two-arm study with 500 patients, administrative censoring, and one competing event (non-cardiovascular death competing with cardiovascular death).


Show code
set.seed(2026)
n <- 500

# Baseline covariates
age <- rnorm(n, mean = 65, sd = 10)
female <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.45)
comorbidity <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.3)

# Treatment assignment (observational, depends on covariates)
ps <- plogis(-1 + 0.02 * age + 0.3 * comorbidity)
A <- rbinom(n, 1, ps)

# Event times (cardiovascular death)
lambda_cv <- exp(-5 + 0.03 * age + 0.5 * comorbidity - 0.4 * A)
T_cv <- rexp(n, rate = lambda_cv)

# Competing event times (non-cardiovascular death)
lambda_noncv <- exp(-5.5 + 0.04 * age + 0.2 * comorbidity - 0.1 * A)
T_noncv <- rexp(n, rate = lambda_noncv)

# Administrative censoring at 5 years
C_admin <- 5

# Observed data
T_obs <- pmin(T_cv, T_noncv, C_admin)
event_type <- ifelse(T_obs == C_admin, 0,       # censored
               ifelse(T_obs == T_cv, 1,          # CV death
                      2))                         # non-CV death

dat <- data.frame(
  age = age,
  female = female,
  comorbidity = comorbidity,
  A = A,
  time = T_obs,
  event = event_type
)

# Summary
table(dat$event)



  0   1   2 
313  86 101 


Show code
summary(dat$time)


    Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
0.001064 2.675169 5.000000 3.919467 5.000000 5.000000 




20.5.2 Naive Kaplan-Meier Estimate
The standard Kaplan-Meier estimator ignores confounding and treats competing events as censoring.


Show code
library(survival)

# Naive KM treating competing events as censored
km_fit <- survfit(Surv(time, event == 1) ~ A, data = dat)

plot(km_fit, col = c("red", "blue"), lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Time (years)", ylab = "Survival probability",
     main = "Naive Kaplan-Meier (ignoring confounding & competing risks)")
legend("bottomleft", c("Control (A=0)", "Treated (A=1)"),
       col = c("red", "blue"), lwd = 2)















Note



This Kaplan-Meier estimate is biased for two reasons: (1) it does not adjust for confounding (age and comorbidity affect both treatment and outcome), and (2) it treats competing events as non-informative censoring.




20.5.3 Cumulative Incidence (Aalen-Johansen)
A better descriptive analysis uses the Aalen-Johansen estimator, which accounts for competing risks but still does not adjust for confounding.


Show code
# Cumulative incidence with competing risks
library(survival)

# Multi-state event indicator
dat$event_ms <- factor(dat$event, levels = 0:2,
                       labels = c("censor", "CV death", "Non-CV death"))

cuminc_fit <- survfit(Surv(time, event_ms) ~ A, data = dat)
plot(cuminc_fit, col = c("red", "blue"), lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Time (years)", ylab = "Cumulative incidence",
     main = "Aalen-Johansen Cumulative Incidence by Treatment")











20.5.4 TMLE for Risk Difference at Time t
We estimate the causal risk difference for cardiovascular death at \(t = 3\) years using a TMLE approach. This adjusts for confounding and can properly handle competing events.


Show code
# --- TMLE for survival: risk difference at t = 3 ---
# This uses a discretized hazard TMLE approach
# Requires: survtmle package (install from GitHub if needed)
# devtools::install_github("benkeser/survtmle")

library(survtmle)

# Discretize time for TMLE (quarterly intervals)
t_target <- 3  # 3-year risk

# Prepare data for survtmle
# Treating competing events as censoring (cause-specific approach)
result <- survtmle(
  ftime   = dat$time,
  ftype   = dat$event,
  trt     = dat$A,
  adjustVars = dat[, c("age", "female", "comorbidity")],
  t0      = t_target,
  SL.ftime = c("SL.glm", "SL.mean"),
  SL.ctime = c("SL.glm", "SL.mean"),
  SL.trt   = c("SL.glm", "SL.mean"),
  method   = "hazard",
  ftypeOfInterest = 1  # CV death
)

# Extract risk difference
print(result)

# Risk under treatment vs control at t = 3
risk_A1 <- result$est[2]  # P(T <= 3 | do(A=1))
risk_A0 <- result$est[1]  # P(T <= 3 | do(A=0))
RD <- risk_A1 - risk_A0

cat(sprintf("Risk under A=1: %.3f\n", risk_A1))
cat(sprintf("Risk under A=0: %.3f\n", risk_A0))
cat(sprintf("Risk difference: %.3f\n", RD))




20.5.5 Diagnostic: Weight Trajectories and Effective Sample Size
Monitoring how IPCW weights behave over time is essential for any survival analysis using inverse weighting.


Show code
# Demonstrate weight instability with a simple IPCW example
# Fit a censoring model
library(survival)

# Create indicators for censoring (event == 0 means administratively censored,
# but we also treat competing events as a form of censoring for this exercise)
dat$censored <- as.integer(dat$event == 0 | dat$event == 2)

# Time grid for diagnostics
time_grid <- seq(0.5, 4.5, by = 0.5)

# At each time point, compute weights for those still at risk
weight_summary <- data.frame(
  time = time_grid,
  n_at_risk = NA,
  mean_weight = NA,
  max_weight = NA,
  ess = NA
)

# Simple time-varying censoring model for illustration
cens_model <- glm(censored ~ age + comorbidity + A,
                  data = dat, family = binomial)
p_uncens <- 1 - predict(cens_model, type = "response")

for (i in seq_along(time_grid)) {
  t_k <- time_grid[i]
  at_risk <- dat$time >= t_k
  n_risk <- sum(at_risk)

  # Simplified cumulative weight (illustrative)
  # In practice, these would be computed from a discrete-time censoring model
  w <- (1 / p_uncens[at_risk])^(i)  # cumulative product approximation
  w <- w / mean(w)  # stabilize

  weight_summary$n_at_risk[i] <- n_risk
  weight_summary$mean_weight[i] <- mean(w)
  weight_summary$max_weight[i] <- max(w)
  weight_summary$ess[i] <- sum(w)^2 / sum(w^2)
}

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

# Plot 1: Max weight over time
plot(weight_summary$time, weight_summary$max_weight, type = "b",
     pch = 19, col = "darkred", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Time (years)", ylab = "Maximum weight",
     main = "Weight instability over time")
abline(h = 10, lty = 2, col = "gray50")
text(1, 10.5, "Concern threshold", col = "gray50", cex = 0.8)

# Plot 2: Effective sample size over time
plot(weight_summary$time, weight_summary$ess, type = "b",
     pch = 19, col = "steelblue", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Time (years)", ylab = "Effective sample size",
     main = "ESS decreases with follow-up")
abline(h = 50, lty = 2, col = "gray50")
text(3, 55, "Minimum ESS threshold", col = "gray50", cex = 0.8)

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))








Figure 20.1: IPCW weight trajectories and effective sample size over time

title: 20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning
text:
20.6 Practical Diagnostics for Survival TMLE

20.6.1 Censoring Model Calibration
Assess whether your censoring model is well-specified by comparing predicted vs. observed censoring rates across risk strata:


Show code
# Calibration of the censoring model
pred_cens <- predict(cens_model, type = "response")
risk_groups <- cut(pred_cens, breaks = quantile(pred_cens, probs = c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1)),
                   include.lowest = TRUE)

cal_table <- aggregate(
  cbind(predicted = pred_cens, observed = dat$censored),
  by = list(risk_group = risk_groups),
  FUN = mean
)
print(cal_table)


     risk_group predicted observed
1 [0.592,0.796] 0.7499236    0.752
2 (0.796,0.835] 0.8163263    0.816
3 (0.835,0.868] 0.8519043    0.856
4 (0.868,0.935] 0.8938457    0.888




20.6.2 Weight Trajectory Plots
For individual-level diagnostics, plot the weight trajectory for a random sample of individuals:


Show code
# In a full implementation, you would extract individual-level weights
# from the survtmle or ltmle output and plot them:
#
# plot(time_points, weights_individual[i, ], type = "l",
#      xlab = "Time", ylab = "IPCW weight",
#      main = "Individual weight trajectories")
#
# Key things to look for:
# - Extreme weights (> 10-20x) suggest positivity violations
# - Rapidly diverging weights suggest model misspecification
# - Weights that "explode" at a specific time point suggest
#   a covariate stratum with very high censoring probability




20.6.3 Effective Sample Size Over Time
The effective sample size (ESS) is the most important summary diagnostic for weighted survival analyses:
\[\text{ESS}(t) = \frac{\left(\sum_{i: T_i \geq t} w_i(t)\right)^2}{\sum_{i: T_i \geq t} w_i(t)^2}\]
Rules of thumb:

ESS < 50 at your target time point: results are unreliable
ESS drops below 10% of nominal sample size: consider shortening follow-up
ESS plot has a sudden drop: investigate what changed at that time point (e.g., a policy change causing differential censoring)
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning

title: 20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning
text:
20.7 Check Your Understanding






Questions




Estimand choice: A colleague proposes using a Cox proportional hazards model to estimate the causal effect of a new drug on overall survival in an observational study. They argue that adjusting for confounders in the Cox model produces a valid causal estimate. What are two problems with this approach, and what estimand would you recommend instead?
Competing risks strategy: In a study of time to kidney transplant, death before transplant is a competing event. A reviewer asks you to justify your choice of estimand. Under what scientific question would you (a) treat death as censoring with IPCW, and (b) use a cumulative incidence approach?
Weight diagnostics: Your IPCW-based survival TMLE shows that the effective sample size drops from 400 to 25 between year 1 and year 3 of follow-up. What does this indicate, and what are two strategies to address it?









Answers






Two problems with the causal HR from a Cox model: (a) Non-collapsibility means the adjusted HR estimates a conditional (within-stratum) quantity that differs from the marginal causal effect, even under correct adjustment. (b) Built-in selection bias at later time points means the hazard comparison is among differentially selected survivors, not the original population. Recommended estimand: the marginal risk difference or RMST difference at a pre-specified time point, estimated using TMLE or IPTW.

Treating death as censoring with IPCW answers: “What would the transplant rate be in a hypothetical world where patients could not die before transplant?” This is useful for understanding the biological mechanism of the treatment on transplant. (b) The cumulative incidence approach answers: “What is the actual probability of receiving a transplant by time t, given that some patients will die first?” This is useful for clinical planning and resource allocation.

The ESS drop indicates severe positivity violations or model misspecification in the censoring model at later time points. The cumulative product of censoring weights is concentrating mass on a few individuals. Strategies: (a) Shorten the analysis time horizon to a point where ESS remains adequate (e.g., report 2-year rather than 3-year results). (b) Truncate extreme weights at a threshold (e.g., the 99th percentile) and report this as a sensitivity analysis, acknowledging the bias-variance tradeoff.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
20  Time-to-Event, Censoring, and Competing Risks with Targeted Learning

Dang, Lauren E, Issa Tager, and Maya L Petersen. 2023. “A Causal Roadmap for Generating High-Quality Real-World Evidence.” Journal of Clinical and Translational Science 7 (1): e127.


Hernán, Miguel A, and James M Robins. 2016. “Using Big Data to Emulate a Target Trial When a Randomized Trial Is Not Available.” American Journal of Epidemiology 183 (8): 758–64.
