23  Transportability and Generalizability in RCT-RWD Synthesis

title: 24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology
text:
25 Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework
Estimated reading time: 75 minutes
Pre-specified, reproducible causal inference for regulatory pharmacoepidemiology
In many regulatory settings, analysts cannot freely explore the data. Post-marketing safety studies, multi-database pharmacoepidemiology collaborations, and distributed data network analyses often require a clean-room workflow: the analysis plan is pre-specified before data access, code is locked, and the analyst has minimal or no opportunity to iterate on model choices after seeing the data.
This is the opposite of typical exploratory data analysis, and it is exactly where TMLE shines.
This chapter demonstrates how to implement TMLE in a clean-room regulatory context, using a motivating example inspired by the NSAIDs vs. opioids acute kidney injury (AKI) safety question.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology

title: 24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology
text:
26.1 The Regulatory Question
A distributed research network (similar to the FDA Sentinel system) has flagged a potential safety signal:

Does initiation of prescription NSAIDs increase the 90-day risk of acute kidney injury compared to initiation of prescription opioids among adults with musculoskeletal pain?

This question arises in a post-marketing surveillance context where:

Raw patient-level data cannot leave participating sites (privacy, legal, or contractual constraints)
The analysis must be pre-specified in a Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP) before any data access
Results must be reproducible: running the same code on the same data yields identical results
The analyst cannot iterate on model specifications after seeing the data
There is an audit trail documenting every analysis step


26.1.1 Key elements

Target population: Adults aged 18-80 with a new musculoskeletal pain diagnosis initiating either NSAIDs or opioids
Treatment: NSAID initiation (A = 1) vs. opioid initiation (A = 0)
Outcome: AKI within 90 days (binary)
Decision: Should the NSAID label carry a stronger AKI warning? Should prescribing guidelines be updated?







Why TMLE for Clean-Room?



Traditional regression-based analyses require the analyst to choose a single model specification, a process that invites data-dependent decisions. Every time an analyst looks at the data and adjusts a model, the pre-specification principle is compromised.
TMLE with Super Learner reduces reliance on a single parametric model while maintaining a pre-specified workflow:

Pre-specify a learner library, not a single model. The SAP lists the candidate algorithms. Cross-validation, not the analyst, picks the best combination. No post-hoc model selection is required.
Double robustness provides consistency under partial misspecification. The TMLE estimate is consistent if at least one of the outcome model or the treatment model is correctly specified (plus positivity). This is a consistency property, not a blanket guarantee; it does not protect against unmeasured confounding or severe positivity violations.
Influence-curve inference follows from the targeting step. Standard errors come directly from the efficient influence curve. When data-adaptive learners are used, cross-fitting or CV-TMLE is needed to ensure the required regularity conditions hold (see Chapter 2.3).
Full determinism. Given the same data, learner library, and random seed, the entire pipeline produces bit-identical results. Every run is reproducible.
The audit trail writes itself. Cross-validated learner weights, the fluctuation parameter, and every diagnostic are logged as natural byproducts of the algorithm.

TMLE does not eliminate the fundamental threats to causal inference: unmeasured confounding, positivity violations, and model extrapolation remain.
What it does is separate the algorithmic decisions (model selection, combination weights) from the substantive decisions (estimand definition, confounder selection, population definition) that must be made before data access. This separation is what makes it well-suited to clean-room workflows.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology

title: 24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology
text:
28.1 Causal Question and Estimand
Before touching any data, we write down exactly what we want to estimate and why.
Question (plain language): What is the 90-day AKI risk if all eligible patients initiated NSAIDs, compared to if all eligible patients initiated opioids?
Estimand: Average Treatment Effect on the risk difference scale:
\[
\psi = E[Y(1)] - E[Y(0)]
\]
where \(Y(1)\) is the potential AKI outcome under NSAID initiation and \(Y(0)\) under opioid initiation.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology

title: 24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology
text:
28.2 Causal Model (DAG)
Confounders (W):
  age, sex, CKD stage, diabetes, hypertension,
  prior NSAID use, prior opioid use, ACE/ARB use,
  number of concomitant medications, healthcare utilization

      W
     / \
    v   v
    A -> Y
All measured confounders \(W\) affect both treatment choice \(A\) and outcome \(Y\). The active comparator design (NSAID vs. opioid) helps control for confounding by indication, since both groups have pain requiring treatment.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology

title: 24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology
text:
28.3 Assumptions
We assume consistency, exchangeability, and positivity as defined in Chapter 1.2. In the clean-room context, these assumptions are locked into the protocol before data access and cannot be revised after seeing results. The application-specific concerns are:

Consistency: “Initiate NSAID” is defined at the drug-class level, assuming within-class variation does not meaningfully affect AKI risk.
Exchangeability: The most fragile assumption. Unmeasured confounders (OTC NSAID use, frailty, kidney function trajectory) could violate it. The E-value sensitivity analysis (Section 8) quantifies how strong such confounding would need to be.
Positivity: Severe CKD patients are rarely prescribed NSAIDs, creating potential near-violations. The propensity score overlap diagnostics will flag this.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology

title: 24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology
text:
28.4 Pre-Specified Analysis Plan
The SAP is the contract between the analyst and the regulator. Once signed, every element below is locked. In a regulatory submission, the analyst cannot go back and re-specify the propensity score model after seeing that it produces extreme weights. The model choice must be locked before data access.






Statistical Analysis Plan: Locked Before Data Access



The following specifications are frozen in the SAP. No modifications are permitted after data access.

Primary estimator: TMLE with Super Learner
Super Learner library:

SL.glm (logistic regression)
SL.glm.interaction (logistic with all two-way interactions)
SL.step (stepwise regression)
SL.mean (intercept-only, for benchmarking)

Number of cross-validation folds: 5
Propensity score bounds: Truncate at [0.025, 0.975]
Secondary estimators: G-computation, IPTW (for comparison)
Pre-specified diagnostics: PS overlap, weight distribution, covariate balance (SMD < 0.1 threshold), EIC mean check
Sensitivity analyses: E-value for unmeasured confounding
Seed: 20260101 (locked in SAP)

Why does each element matter? The learner library determines what models the algorithm can consider. The CV folds control the bias-variance trade-off in model selection. The PS bounds prevent extreme weights. The diagnostics confirm that the estimation procedure is operating in a regime where we trust it. And the seed ensures anyone can reproduce the exact numerical result.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology

title: 24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology
text:
30.1 Step 1: Propensity Score Estimation
Before we can estimate the treatment effect, we need to model the treatment assignment mechanism. The propensity score answers the question: given a patient’s baseline characteristics, how likely were they to receive NSAIDs rather than opioids?






Step 1: Estimate the Propensity Score



The propensity score \(g(W) = P(A = 1 \mid W)\) estimates each patient’s probability of receiving NSAIDs given their baseline covariates. We need this for two reasons:

It allows us to construct the clever covariate that targets the TMLE update toward the causal parameter of interest.
It helps us assess positivity, that is, whether both treatments are plausible for all patients.

The model formula and truncation bounds were locked in the SAP before data access. Truncation at [0.025, 0.975] prevents extreme inverse-probability weights from individual observations dominating the estimate.



# ============================================================
# PRE-SPECIFIED: Propensity score model
# SAP Section 4.2: Use logistic regression with all baseline
# covariates, squared age term, and CKD indicators
# ============================================================

covariates <- c("age", "male", "ckd", "diabetes", "hypertension",
                "prior_nsaid", "prior_opioid", "ace_arb", "n_rx", "n_visits")

# Convert CKD to factor for proper modeling
dat <- dat %>%
  mutate(ckd_f = factor(ckd))

g_formula <- A ~ age + I(age^2) + male + ckd_f + diabetes + hypertension +
  prior_nsaid + prior_opioid + ace_arb + n_rx + n_visits +
  prior_nsaid:ckd_f + ace_arb:diabetes

g_mod <- glm(g_formula, family = binomial, data = dat)

dat$ps <- predict(g_mod, type = "response")

# PRE-SPECIFIED: Truncate at [0.025, 0.975]
dat$ps_trunc <- pmax(0.025, pmin(0.975, dat$ps))
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology

title: 24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology
text:
30.2 Step 2: Pre-Specified Diagnostics (Covariate Balance)
These diagnostics are run before looking at the effect estimate, to confirm that the propensity score model is adequate. Think of this as a quality gate: if the diagnostics fail, we know the estimation is unreliable regardless of what number comes out.

30.2.1 Propensity score overlap
The overlap plot is the most important single diagnostic. If the two treatment groups have propensity score distributions that do not overlap, we are trying to compare patients who are fundamentally different, and no statistical method can rescue that.






What to Look For: Propensity Score Overlap



A healthy overlap plot shows two distributions that cover a similar range, with substantial overlap in the middle. Warning signs include:

Separation: If the two curves barely overlap, positivity is violated and the estimate will rely heavily on extrapolation.
Spikes near 0 or 1: Patients with propensity scores near the boundaries contribute extreme weights. The truncation at [0.025, 0.975] mitigates this, but if many patients are truncated, the effective estimand changes.
Asymmetry: If one group is concentrated in a narrow range while the other is spread out, consider whether the target population needs to be restricted.




# ============================================================
# DIAGNOSTIC 1: Propensity score overlap
# SAP Section 5.1
# ============================================================

ggplot(dat, aes(x = ps, fill = factor(A, labels = c("Opioid", "NSAID")))) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45) +
  labs(
    x = "Estimated propensity score P(NSAID | W)",
    y = "Density",
    fill = "Treatment",
    title = "Diagnostic 1: Propensity Score Overlap"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#4477AA", "#EE6677"))









# Quantile summary
dat %>%
  group_by(Treatment = factor(A, labels = c("Opioid", "NSAID"))) %>%
  summarise(
    min  = round(min(ps), 3),
    p5   = round(quantile(ps, 0.05), 3),
    p25  = round(quantile(ps, 0.25), 3),
    median = round(median(ps), 3),
    p75  = round(quantile(ps, 0.75), 3),
    p95  = round(quantile(ps, 0.95), 3),
    max  = round(max(ps), 3),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
#> # A tibble: 2 × 8
#>   Treatment   min    p5   p25 median   p75   p95   max
#>   <fct>     <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
#> 1 Opioid    0.075 0.202 0.328  0.447 0.561 0.733 0.841
#> 2 NSAID     0.077 0.287 0.459  0.564 0.667 0.801 0.841



30.2.2 Covariate balance: Standardized Mean Differences






What to Look For: Covariate Balance



Standardized Mean Differences (SMDs) measure how different the treatment groups are on each covariate. The SAP pre-specifies a threshold of SMD < 0.1 for adequate balance.

Unadjusted SMDs show the raw imbalance in the data. Large values confirm confounding is present.
Weighted SMDs show the imbalance after IPTW adjustment. If all weighted SMDs fall below 0.1, the propensity score model has successfully balanced the groups on observed covariates.
The Love plot visualizes this comparison. Look for all weighted points (green) to fall left of the dashed 0.1 threshold line.

If weighted SMDs remain above 0.1 for important confounders, the propensity score model needs refinement. In a clean-room analysis, this would have been caught in the simulation phase and the model adjusted before locking the code.



# ============================================================
# DIAGNOSTIC 2: Covariate balance before and after weighting
# SAP Section 5.2: SMD threshold < 0.1
# ============================================================

compute_smd <- function(data, var, trt, weights = NULL) {
  if (is.null(weights)) weights <- rep(1, nrow(data))
  d1 <- data[[var]][data[[trt]] == 1]
  d0 <- data[[var]][data[[trt]] == 0]
  w1 <- weights[data[[trt]] == 1]
  w0 <- weights[data[[trt]] == 0]
  m1 <- weighted.mean(d1, w1)
  m0 <- weighted.mean(d0, w0)
  s1 <- sqrt(sum(w1 * (d1 - m1)^2) / sum(w1))
  s0 <- sqrt(sum(w0 * (d0 - m0)^2) / sum(w0))
  pooled_sd <- sqrt((s1^2 + s0^2) / 2)
  if (pooled_sd == 0) return(0)
  (m1 - m0) / pooled_sd
}

# Stabilized IPTW weights for balance assessment
p_A <- mean(dat$A)
dat$sw <- ifelse(dat$A == 1,
                 p_A / dat$ps_trunc,
                 (1 - p_A) / (1 - dat$ps_trunc))

balance_vars <- c("age", "male", "ckd", "diabetes", "hypertension",
                  "prior_nsaid", "prior_opioid", "ace_arb", "n_rx", "n_visits")

smd_raw <- sapply(balance_vars, function(v) compute_smd(dat, v, "A"))
smd_wt  <- sapply(balance_vars, function(v) compute_smd(dat, v, "A", dat$sw))

balance_df <- tibble(
  Covariate  = balance_vars,
  Unadjusted = round(abs(smd_raw), 3),
  Weighted   = round(abs(smd_wt), 3)
)

balance_df
#> # A tibble: 10 × 3
#>    Covariate    Unadjusted Weighted
#>    <chr>             <dbl>    <dbl>
#>  1 age               0.363    0.005
#>  2 male              0.086    0.005
#>  3 ckd               0.344    0.004
#>  4 diabetes          0.204    0.001
#>  5 hypertension      0.162    0.003
#>  6 prior_nsaid       0.373    0.008
#>  7 prior_opioid      0.076    0.009
#>  8 ace_arb           0.165    0.005
#>  9 n_rx              0.283    0.009
#> 10 n_visits          0.225    0.005


# Love plot
balance_long <- balance_df %>%
  pivot_longer(-Covariate, names_to = "Method", values_to = "SMD")

ggplot(balance_long, aes(x = SMD, y = reorder(Covariate, SMD),
                         color = Method, shape = Method)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  labs(
    x = "Absolute Standardized Mean Difference",
    y = "",
    title = "Diagnostic 2: Covariate Balance (Love Plot)",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = 0.1 threshold from SAP"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#EE6677", "#228833"))










30.2.3 Weight distribution

# ============================================================
# DIAGNOSTIC 3: Weight distribution
# SAP Section 5.3
# ============================================================

ggplot(dat, aes(x = sw, fill = factor(A, labels = c("Opioid", "NSAID")))) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, alpha = 0.6, position = "identity") +
  labs(
    x = "Stabilized IPTW weight",
    y = "Count",
    fill = "Treatment",
    title = "Diagnostic 3: Weight Distribution"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#4477AA", "#EE6677")) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40")








cat("Weight summary:\n")
#> Weight summary:
summary(dat$sw)
#>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
#>  0.5361  0.7450  0.8946  1.0013  1.1163  6.5225
cat("Fraction > 5:", round(mean(dat$sw > 5), 4), "\n")
#> Fraction > 5: 4e-04
cat("Fraction > 10:", round(mean(dat$sw > 10), 4), "\n")
#> Fraction > 10: 0
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology

title: 24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology
text:
30.3 Step 3: Primary Estimator, TMLE (by hand)
With diagnostics confirming adequate overlap and balance, we proceed to estimation. TMLE is a multi-step algorithm. We walk through each step with a plain-language explanation, then the code. If you have read the KH Stats TMLE tutorial, you will recognize the same logic here, just applied in a clean-room regulatory context.






Step 3a: Fit the Initial Outcome Model (Q)



First, we build a model to predict the outcome (AKI) from treatment and covariates. This is called the “initial Q,” our first rough guess at each patient’s risk under each treatment scenario.
We fit one model and then generate three sets of predictions:

Q1: predicted risk if the patient had received NSAIDs (set A = 1 for everyone)
Q0: predicted risk if the patient had received opioids (set A = 0 for everyone)
QA: predicted risk under the treatment they actually received

These predictions are “initial” because TMLE will update them in the targeting step to reduce bias for the specific causal parameter we care about.



# ============================================================
# PRIMARY ANALYSIS: TMLE
# SAP Section 6.1
# ============================================================

logit <- function(p) log(p / (1 - p))

# --- Step 3a: Initial outcome model ---
# Pre-specified model form from SAP
q_formula <- Y ~ A + age + I(age^2) + male + ckd_f + diabetes + hypertension +
  prior_nsaid + prior_opioid + ace_arb + n_rx + n_visits +
  A:ckd_f + A:ace_arb + A:diabetes

q_mod <- glm(q_formula, family = binomial, data = dat)

# Initial predictions
Q1_init <- predict(q_mod, newdata = dat %>% mutate(A = 1), type = "response")
Q0_init <- predict(q_mod, newdata = dat %>% mutate(A = 0), type = "response")
QA_init <- predict(q_mod, type = "response")

cat("Initial outcome model fitted.\n")
#> Initial outcome model fitted.
cat("Q1 range:", round(range(Q1_init), 4), "\n")
#> Q1 range: 0.0084 0.7659
cat("Q0 range:", round(range(Q0_init), 4), "\n")
#> Q0 range: 0.0045 0.5594







Step 3b: Compute the Clever Covariate



The clever covariate bridges the outcome model and the treatment model. It tells the fluctuation step where to adjust the initial predictions and by how much.
For the ATE, the clever covariate for each observation is:
\[H(A, W) = \frac{A}{g(W)} - \frac{1-A}{1-g(W)}\]
For treated patients (A = 1), this simplifies to \(1/g(W)\), the inverse of their probability of being treated. For controls (A = 0), it is \(-1/(1 - g(W))\). Patients who received an unlikely treatment get a larger clever covariate, meaning the targeting step will adjust their predictions more. This is the mechanism through which TMLE “borrows strength” from the propensity score to correct the outcome model.



# --- Step 3b: Clever covariate ---
H_A <- dat$A / dat$ps_trunc - (1 - dat$A) / (1 - dat$ps_trunc)
H_1 <- 1 / dat$ps_trunc
H_0 <- -1 / (1 - dat$ps_trunc)

cat("Clever covariate range:", round(range(H_A), 2), "\n")
#> Clever covariate range: -6.28 12.93







Step 3c: Fluctuation (Targeting) Step



This is the step that makes TMLE special. We fit a simple logistic regression with:

Outcome: the observed Y
Offset: the logit of our initial predictions (QA_init)
Covariate: the clever covariate H(A, W)
No intercept (-1 in the formula)

The single coefficient from this regression, called epsilon, tells us how much and in which direction to shift our initial predictions. The offset locks in the initial predictions; epsilon only adjusts them in the direction that reduces bias for the ATE specifically.
This is the “targeting” in Targeted Maximum Likelihood: the initial model is updated to be optimal for our specific estimand, not just for prediction in general.



# --- Step 3c: Fluctuation (targeting) model ---
fluc <- glm(dat$Y ~ -1 + offset(logit(QA_init)) + H_A,
            family = binomial)
epsilon <- coef(fluc)

cat("Epsilon (fluctuation parameter):", round(epsilon, 5), "\n")
#> Epsilon (fluctuation parameter): -0.00741







Step 3d-e: Update Predictions and Compute the Final TMLE Estimate



Now we apply the epsilon update to get our final, targeted predictions:

Shift Q1_init and Q0_init on the logit scale by epsilon times their respective clever covariates
Transform back to the probability scale using the inverse-logit (plogis)
Average the updated predictions across all patients to get the counterfactual risks
Take the difference to get the ATE

The resulting estimate, \(\hat{\psi}_{TMLE}\), targets the pre-specified estimand: the 90-day AKI risk difference under universal NSAID versus universal opioid initiation. It is doubly robust (consistent if either the outcome model or the propensity score model is correctly specified) and solves the efficient influence curve estimating equation, which means it achieves the smallest possible variance among regular asymptotically linear estimators under appropriate regularity conditions.



# --- Step 3d: Updated predictions ---
Q1_star <- plogis(logit(Q1_init) + epsilon * H_1)
Q0_star <- plogis(logit(Q0_init) + epsilon * H_0)

# --- Step 3e: TMLE estimate ---
tmle_risk1 <- mean(Q1_star)
tmle_risk0 <- mean(Q0_star)
tmle_ate   <- tmle_risk1 - tmle_risk0

cat("TMLE risk (NSAIDs): ", round(tmle_risk1, 4), "\n")
#> TMLE risk (NSAIDs):  0.0912
cat("TMLE risk (opioids):", round(tmle_risk0, 4), "\n")
#> TMLE risk (opioids): 0.0512
cat("TMLE ATE:           ", round(tmle_ate, 4), "\n")
#> TMLE ATE:            0.04
cat("True ATE:           ", round(true_ate, 4), "\n")
#> True ATE:            0.0334


# --- Step 3f: Inference via efficient influence curve ---
eic <- (dat$A / dat$ps_trunc) * (dat$Y - Q1_star) + Q1_star - tmle_risk1 -
       ((1 - dat$A) / (1 - dat$ps_trunc)) * (dat$Y - Q0_star) - Q0_star + tmle_risk0

tmle_se <- sqrt(var(eic) / n)
tmle_ci <- tmle_ate + c(-1.96, 1.96) * tmle_se

cat("\n=== PRIMARY RESULT ===\n")
#> 
#> === PRIMARY RESULT ===
cat("TMLE ATE:", round(tmle_ate, 4), "\n")
#> TMLE ATE: 0.04
cat("SE:      ", round(tmle_se, 4), "\n")
#> SE:       0.0064
cat("95% CI:  [", round(tmle_ci[1], 4), ",", round(tmle_ci[2], 4), "]\n")
#> 95% CI:  [ 0.0275 , 0.0525 ]
cat("p-value: ", round(2 * pnorm(-abs(tmle_ate / tmle_se)), 4), "\n")
#> p-value:  0


# --- Step 3g: Verify EIC mean ≈ 0 ---
cat("EIC mean:", round(mean(eic), 8), " (should be ~0)\n")
#> EIC mean: 0  (should be ~0)







Why Is the EIC Mean Zero?



The efficient influence curve (EIC) for the TMLE estimate should have a mean of approximately zero. This is not a coincidence; it is a direct consequence of the targeting step.
When we fit the fluctuation model with the clever covariate, the score equation of that logistic regression forces the EIC estimating equation to be solved. In other words, the targeting step guarantees that the mean of the EIC is (numerically) zero.
Why this matters for clean-room analysis: The EIC mean is a built-in diagnostic. If it deviates meaningfully from zero, something went wrong in the implementation, perhaps a coding error, numerical overflow in the logit transformation, or a problem with the propensity score truncation. In a locked analysis where you cannot inspect intermediate results, having an automatic self-check is invaluable.
What “approximately zero” means in practice: Machine precision means you will see values like 1e-15 to 1e-8. Values larger than 1e-4 warrant investigation. Values larger than 1e-2 indicate a serious problem.
crumbs: Part V: Special Topics
24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology

title: 24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology
text:
30.4 Step 4: Secondary Estimators (for comparison)
The SAP pre-specifies secondary estimators so that the primary TMLE result can be compared for consistency. If all three estimators agree, this strengthens confidence in the result. If they disagree, the TMLE estimate is preferred due to its double robustness, but the discrepancy is documented.

30.4.1 G-computation

# ============================================================
# SECONDARY ANALYSIS 1: G-computation
# SAP Section 6.2
# ============================================================

gcomp_risk1 <- mean(Q1_init)
gcomp_risk0 <- mean(Q0_init)
gcomp_ate   <- gcomp_risk1 - gcomp_risk0

cat("G-computation ATE:", round(gcomp_ate, 4), "\n")
#> G-computation ATE: 0.0423



30.4.2 IPTW

# ============================================================
# SECONDARY ANALYSIS 2: IPTW with stabilized weights
# SAP Section 6.3
# ============================================================

risk1_iptw <- with(dat, sum(sw * Y * (A == 1)) / sum(sw * (A == 1)))
risk0_iptw <- with(dat, sum(sw * Y * (A == 0)) / sum(sw * (A == 0)))
iptw_ate   <- risk1_iptw - risk0_iptw

cat("IPTW ATE:", round(iptw_ate, 4), "\n")
#> IPTW ATE: 0.0408



30.4.3 Comparison table

# ============================================================
# RESULTS COMPARISON TABLE
# SAP Section 7
# ============================================================

results <- tibble(
  Estimator = c("Unadjusted", "G-computation", "IPTW", "TMLE"),
  ATE = round(c(
    mean(dat$Y[dat$A == 1]) - mean(dat$Y[dat$A == 0]),
    gcomp_ate,
    iptw_ate,
    tmle_ate
  ), 4),
  SE = round(c(NA, NA, NA, tmle_se), 4),
  CI_lower = round(c(NA, NA, NA, tmle_ci[1]), 4),
  CI_upper = round(c(NA, NA, NA, tmle_ci[2]), 4)
)

results
#> # A tibble: 4 × 5
#>   Estimator        ATE      SE CI_lower CI_upper
#>   <chr>          <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
#> 1 Unadjusted    0.0041 NA       NA       NA     
#> 2 G-computation 0.0423 NA       NA       NA     
#> 3 IPTW          0.0408 NA       NA       NA     
#> 4 TMLE          0.04    0.0064   0.0275   0.0525

text:
30.5 Step 5: Post-Estimation Diagnostics

30.5.1 Clever covariate distribution

# ============================================================
# DIAGNOSTIC 4: Clever covariate ranges
# SAP Section 5.4
# ============================================================

cc_df <- tibble(
  H = H_A,
  Treatment = factor(dat$A, labels = c("Opioid", "NSAID"))
)

ggplot(cc_df, aes(x = H, fill = Treatment)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, alpha = 0.5, position = "identity") +
  labs(
    x = "Clever covariate H(A, W)",
    y = "Count",
    fill = "Treatment",
    title = "Diagnostic 4: Clever Covariate Distribution"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#4477AA", "#EE6677"))










30.5.2 Influence curve stability

# ============================================================
# DIAGNOSTIC 5: Influence curve distribution
# SAP Section 5.5
# ============================================================

ggplot(tibble(eic = eic), aes(x = eic)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "#228833", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(
    x = "Efficient influence curve value",
    y = "Count",
    title = "Diagnostic 5: EIC Distribution"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








cat("EIC summary:\n")
#> EIC summary:
summary(eic)
#>     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
#> -8.35531 -0.09618 -0.04799  0.00000  0.07999  4.88995
cat("\nTop 5 most influential observations:\n")
#> 
#> Top 5 most influential observations:
head(sort(abs(eic), decreasing = TRUE), 5)
#>       37     4648     3572      501     5800 
#> 8.355309 4.889952 4.527676 4.280250 4.274389



30.5.3 Predicted risk distribution

# ============================================================
# DIAGNOSTIC 6: Predicted outcome distributions
# SAP Section 5.6
# ============================================================

pred_df <- tibble(
  Q1_star = Q1_star,
  Q0_star = Q0_star,
  diff = Q1_star - Q0_star
)

ggplot(pred_df, aes(x = diff)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#CC6677", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean(pred_df$diff), linetype = "solid", color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = true_ate, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(
    x = "Individual-level risk difference (Q1* - Q0*)",
    y = "Count",
    title = "Diagnostic 6: Distribution of Individual Treatment Effects",
    subtitle = "Black line = TMLE ATE; Red dashed = True ATE"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

  cat("assessment, particularly for patients with pre-existing CKD.\n")
} else if (tmle_ci[2] < 0) {

} else {

}

section: 34.2 How TMLE supports the regulatory decision
text:
34.2 How TMLE supports the regulatory decision







Regulatory concern
How TMLE addresses it




Pre-specification required
SAP locks the learner library, seed, and analysis code


Must be reproducible
Deterministic given data + seed + code


Model selection bias
Super Learner eliminates manual model selection


Robustness to misspecification
Double robustness: consistency under correct specification of at least one nuisance model (plus positivity)


Valid inference under regularity conditions
Influence-curve standard errors do not require bootstrap


Auditable
Cross-validated learner weights, diagnostics, and the fluctuation parameter are all logged


Transparent
Every step (initial model, clever covariate, targeting, inference) has a clear purpose

title: 24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology
text:
34.3 What assumptions are most fragile?

Unmeasured confounding: OTC NSAID use is not captured in claims data. Frailty and functional status are poorly measured. The E-value provides a benchmark for how strong this confounding would need to be.
Positivity in CKD subgroups: Patients with severe CKD are rarely prescribed NSAIDs. The propensity score overlap diagnostic should flag this. If violated, consider restricting to the subpopulation with adequate overlap or using stochastic interventions.
Outcome misclassification: AKI may be under-coded in claims data. This affects interpretation but not the validity of the TMLE procedure.

title: 24  Chapter 3.8: TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework for Pharmacoepidemiology
text:
36.2 Software Implementation (R)
This clean-room analysis script demonstrates a fully prespecified TMLE workflow: every modeling choice is locked before running, a fixed seed and library are declared, and the analysis is self-documenting. This is the discipline advocated in this chapter.

All choices documented before execution: estimand, learner library, positivity bounds
Uses a fixed Super Learner library (no data-peeking)
Captures sessionInfo() for full reproducibility
Single tmle() call with prespecified parameters


## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
## CLEAN-ROOM TMLE ANALYSIS SCRIPT
## Prespecified analysis - no data-adaptive choices below
## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

## ── 0. Lock random seed and declare estimand ──
set.seed(1)
ESTIMAND   <- "ATE (risk difference)"
POPULATION <- "Adults with simulated cardiovascular risk factors"
TREATMENT  <- "Drug A vs Drug B"

## ── 1. Prespecified learner libraries ──
Q_LIBRARY  <- c("SL.glm")   # outcome model
G_LIBRARY  <- c("SL.glm")   # treatment model
G_BOUNDS   <- c(0.025, 0.975)  # positivity truncation

## ── 2. Simulate data (replace with real data read) ──
n <- 600
W1 <- rnorm(n)
W2 <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.35)
W3 <- rnorm(n, mean = 0.3 * W1)
A  <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-0.2 + 0.5 * W1 + 0.4 * W2))
Y  <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-1.5 + 0.5 * A + 0.6 * W1 + 0.7 * W2 + 0.2 * W3))

W <- data.frame(W1 = W1, W2 = W2, W3 = W3)

## ── 3. Run prespecified analysis ──
if (requireNamespace("tmle", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(tmle)

  fit <- tmle(
    Y = Y, A = A, W = W,
    family = "binomial",
    Q.SL.library = Q_LIBRARY,
    g.SL.library = G_LIBRARY,
    gbound = G_BOUNDS[1]
  )

  ## ── 4. Report results ──
  cat("══════════════════════════════════════\n")
  cat("CLEAN-ROOM TMLE RESULTS\n")
  cat("══════════════════════════════════════\n")
  cat("Estimand:", ESTIMAND, "\n")
  cat("Population:", POPULATION, "\n")
  cat("Treatment:", TREATMENT, "\n")
  cat("──────────────────────────────────────\n")
  cat("ATE:    ", round(fit$estimates$ATE$psi, 4), "\n")
  cat("95% CI:", round(fit$estimates$ATE$CI, 4), "\n")
  cat("p-value:", format.pval(fit$estimates$ATE$pvalue, digits = 3), "\n")
  cat("──────────────────────────────────────\n")
  cat("Q library:", paste(Q_LIBRARY, collapse = ", "), "\n")
  cat("g library:", paste(G_LIBRARY, collapse = ", "), "\n")
  cat("g-bounds: [", G_BOUNDS[1], ",", G_BOUNDS[2], "]\n")
  cat("══════════════════════════════════════\n")

  ## EIC diagnostic
  cat("EIC mean:", round(mean(fit$estimates$ATE$IC), 6), "\n")
} else {
  message("Install the 'tmle' package:  install.packages('tmle')")
}

## ── 5. Session info for reproducibility ──
cat("\n-- Session info --\n")
sessionInfo()

The clean-room framework pre-specifies every modeling choice. But sometimes the scientific question is not just about the total effect of a treatment. The next chapter introduces mediation analysis with TMLE, decomposing total effects into direct and indirect pathways.

title: 25  Lessons from the Antiretroviral Therapy Case Study
text:
25.1 What a naive analysis might have done
Estimated reading time: 10 minutes
Throughout this handbook, we have used the longitudinal HIV antiretroviral therapy cohort to illustrate every major concept, from defining causal questions to diagnosing estimator behavior (Petersen and Laan 2014; Hernán and Robins 2025). See the Running Case Study for the full data description, including the variable dictionary and target trial protocol. This final section draws together the lessons learned across all preceding chapters and highlights the progression from naive methods to principled causal estimation.
The haartdat dataset contains 1,200 HIV-positive patients observed in 100-day intervals. The key variables are the time-varying treatment indicator (haartind), square-root CD4 count (cd4.sqrt), death indicator (event), censoring flag (dropout), and patient identifier (patient). Time is indexed by tstart and fuptime, with baseline covariates age and sex.
A researcher approaching this dataset without the Causal Roadmap would likely:
As we saw in the running case study introduction, these naive approaches can make an effective treatment appear harmful or produce effect estimates that are difficult to interpret clinically.
crumbs: Synthesis
25  Lessons from the Antiretroviral Therapy Case Study

title: 25  Lessons from the Antiretroviral Therapy Case Study
text:
Start with whatever model seemed natural. A Cox regression or pooled logistic regression, adjusting for available covariates, and report the hazard ratio or odds ratio as “the effect of HAART.”
Adjust for time-varying CD4 counts in the regression. This seems like the right thing to do, since CD4 is a confounder. But because CD4 is also affected by prior treatment, conditioning on it blocks part of the causal pathway and introduces collider-stratification bias.
Report a conditional coefficient on the wrong scale. Even if the model were correct, the hazard ratio or conditional odds ratio does not answer the question “how many deaths would be prevented if everyone initiated HAART?”
crumbs: Synthesis
25  Lessons from the Antiretroviral Therapy Case Study

title: 25  Lessons from the Antiretroviral Therapy Case Study
section: 25.2 How the Causal Roadmap clarified the question
text:
25.2 How the Causal Roadmap clarified the question
The Causal Roadmap forced us to be explicit at every step:

Step 1 (Target Trial Protocol). We defined the causal question precisely: among HIV-positive patients at seroconversion, what is the effect on mortality of initiating HAART compared to not initiating, with follow-up from seroconversion?
Step 2 (Causal Model). We drew a DAG showing that CD4 count is both a confounder (it affects HAART initiation) and a mediator (HAART affects CD4, which affects mortality). This immediately flagged the treatment-confounder feedback problem.
Step 3 (Estimand). We chose the marginal risk difference in mortality, a population-level quantity directly relevant to clinical decision-making.
Step 4 (Identification). We stated the assumptions (exchangeability, consistency, positivity) and used the DAG to argue which variables were sufficient for identification. We also identified where these assumptions might fail (unmeasured confounding from adherence or clinical staging).
Steps 5-6 (Estimation and Evaluation). We applied g-computation, IPTW, and TMLE, compared their results, and used diagnostics to assess credibility.
crumbs: Synthesis
25  Lessons from the Antiretroviral Therapy Case Study

title: 25  Lessons from the Antiretroviral Therapy Case Study
section: 25.3 Why time-dependent confounding required longitudinal methods
text:
25.3 Why time-dependent confounding required longitudinal methods
The central lesson of this case study is that standard regression cannot handle treatment-confounder feedback. When a time-varying confounder (cd4.sqrt) is both a consequence of prior treatment (haartind) and a cause of future treatment, the analyst faces an impossible choice in a regression framework:

Do not adjust for time-varying CD4: leaves confounding of future treatment unaddressed.
Adjust for time-varying CD4: blocks part of the treatment effect and introduces collider bias.

Neither option produces a consistent estimate of the causal effect, as demonstrated concretely in Chapter 3.7. The g-methods (g-computation, IPTW with marginal structural models, longitudinal TMLE) resolve this dilemma by modeling the treatment mechanism separately from the outcome and using that model to reweight or standardize the analysis. Chapter 3.9 walked through the mechanics of longitudinal TMLE step by step, showing how backwards sequential regression conditions on cd4.sqrt where it confounds future treatment and integrates it out where it mediates past treatment. This is not a minor technical improvement; it is a qualitative change in what can be estimated.
crumbs: Synthesis
25  Lessons from the Antiretroviral Therapy Case Study

title: 25  Lessons from the Antiretroviral Therapy Case Study
section: 25.4 What the example illustrates about real-world evidence analyses
text:
25.4 What the example illustrates about real-world evidence analyses
The HIV/HAART example is not unique. Treatment-confounder feedback arises whenever:

Treatment decisions respond to time-varying patient status (lab values, symptoms, disease progression)
Those same patient characteristics are also affected by prior treatment
The outcome of interest occurs at the end of a follow-up period during which both treatment and confounders evolve

This pattern is ubiquitous in chronic disease epidemiology, pharmacoepidemiology, and comparative effectiveness research. Examples include:

Insulin dose adjustments based on HbA1c in diabetes
Immunosuppressant dosing based on kidney function in transplant recipients
Chemotherapy regimen changes based on tumor markers in oncology
Blood pressure medication titration based on BP readings

In all of these settings, the methods covered in this handbook, not standard regression, are needed to estimate causal effects correctly. The Causal Roadmap provides the structure, and the estimation methods (g-computation, IPTW, TMLE, longitudinal TMLE) provide the tools.
crumbs: Synthesis
25  Lessons from the Antiretroviral Therapy Case Study

title: 25  Lessons from the Antiretroviral Therapy Case Study
text:
25.5 Summary

source("_data/load-haart-cohort.R")

# Final summary table of approaches and their limitations
tibble::tribble(
  ~Approach, ~`Handles confounding`, ~`Handles feedback`, ~`Marginal effect`, ~`Valid inference`,
  "Crude comparison",          "No",  "No",  "Yes", "No",

Even after applying longitudinal methods, the results depend on untestable identification assumptions. Chapter 3.12 showed how to probe this dependence using E-values, quantitative bias analysis, and truncation sensitivity. For the HAART cohort, unmeasured adherence is the most plausible threat: patients who adhere to treatment tend to be healthier, potentially inflating the apparent benefit. Sensitivity analysis quantifies how strong this confounding would need to be to explain away the estimated effect.
The progression from naive regression to longitudinal TMLE is not just methodological sophistication for its own sake. Each step solves a real problem that the previous approach could not handle. The antiretroviral therapy case study makes those problems, and their solutions, concrete.

title: 26  Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them
text:
26.1 Pitfall: Confusing Association With Causation
Estimated reading time: 50 minutes
A practical guide for avoiding frequent mistakes in causal inference and targeted learning
Even with a solid understanding of the Causal Roadmap and modern estimators, it is easy to make analytic choices that lead to biased, unstable, or misleading results. In real-world evidence (RWE), the stakes are higher: data are messy, treatment pathways are irregular, and credibility depends on careful alignment between the scientific question, identification conditions, and the estimator.
This chapter catalogs common pitfalls encountered in causal inference, especially in pharmacoepidemiologic and longitudinal RWE settings, and provides practical strategies to avoid them. Each pitfall is organized around a common mistake, an explanation of why it matters, and concrete guidance for avoidance. Where possible, we connect the pitfall to the target trial emulation framework and the estimand, since most errors can be traced to a breakdown in the chain from question to design to identification to estimation.
crumbs: Appendices
26  Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

title: 26  Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them
text:
26.1.1 The regression coefficient problem
Mistake: Fitting a multivariable regression and interpreting the treatment coefficient as a causal effect.
Why it matters: Regression coefficients are typically conditional associations, not causal contrasts. Unless the model corresponds to the identified statistical functional and is correctly specified, the result may not estimate the causal estimand. In pharmacoepidemiology, this often manifests when an analyst fits a Cox model with treatment as a covariate alongside confounders and interprets the hazard ratio as the causal effect of treatment. The coefficient reflects neither the marginal causal effect nor any well-defined intervention, particularly when the model includes variables on the causal pathway or when the hazard ratio is non-collapsible.
RWE example. A study of statin initiation and cardiovascular events fits a multivariable Cox model adjusting for age, sex, LDL cholesterol, diabetes, and smoking status. The analyst reports the adjusted hazard ratio as the “effect of statins.” However, if LDL is a mediator (statins lower LDL, which reduces cardiovascular risk), the coefficient does not correspond to the total causal effect. If the model is misspecified (e.g., the true relationship between age and the log-hazard is nonlinear), the coefficient is biased even for the conditional association.
Avoid by: - Defining a precise causal question and estimand before fitting any model. Use the estimand specification template above. - Estimating the corresponding statistical functional using g-computation, IPTW, AIPW, or TMLE, which explicitly separate the causal target from the nuisance estimation strategy. - Using flexible nuisance estimation (for example, SuperLearner) where appropriate, so that the result does not depend on a single parametric specification. - Drawing a DAG to determine which variables are confounders (must adjust), mediators (must not adjust for total effect), and colliders (must not condition on).
Diagnostics and sensitivity analysis. Compare the regression-based estimate with a properly identified estimate (e.g., TMLE with the same covariate set). If the two diverge substantially, investigate whether model misspecification, non-collapsibility, or mediator adjustment explains the difference. As a robustness check, vary the functional form assumptions in the regression (e.g., add splines for continuous confounders) and assess stability.
crumbs: Appendices
26  Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

title: 26  Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them
text:
26.2 Pitfall: Adjusting for Post-Treatment Variables

26.2.1 The mediator/collider trap
Mistake: Including variables measured after treatment initiation in outcome regression or propensity score models.
Why it matters: - Post-treatment variables may lie on the causal pathway (mediators), so adjustment can block part of the effect - They may be colliders, inducing spurious associations - Adjustment can invalidate the intervention being emulated
This pitfall is directly connected to the target trial protocol. In a well-specified target trial, the adjustment set consists of baseline covariates measured at or before time zero. Any variable measured after time zero is, by definition, post-treatment and should not appear in the propensity score or baseline outcome model unless the analysis explicitly targets a mediation estimand or uses longitudinal methods designed for time-varying confounding.
RWE example. A study of biologic therapy for rheumatoid arthritis adjusts for C-reactive protein (CRP) measured three months after treatment initiation. CRP is affected by treatment (biologics reduce inflammation) and also predicts joint destruction (the outcome). Adjusting for three-month CRP blocks the indirect pathway through inflammation reduction, attenuating the estimated total effect. If the analyst instead wants the direct effect not mediated by CRP, that is a different estimand requiring a different identification strategy (e.g., natural direct effect estimation via mediation analysis).
Decision rule for time-varying confounding. If a covariate is (a) affected by prior treatment and (b) also a confounder of subsequent treatment and the outcome, then baseline-only adjustment is insufficient. This is the hallmark of time-varying confounding affected by prior treatment, and it requires longitudinal methods: IPTW for marginal structural models, longitudinal g-computation, or LTMLE. These methods adjust for the confounding without blocking the causal pathway, by modeling the treatment and censoring mechanisms at each time point rather than conditioning on intermediate values.
Avoid by: - Restricting adjustment to baseline confounders for baseline treatment effects, as specified in the target trial protocol. - Using longitudinal methods (for example, LTMLE or LMTP) when time-varying confounding affected by prior treatment is present. LTMLE targets the G-formula under sequential exchangeability, estimating the counterfactual outcome under sustained treatment strategies while respecting the temporal ordering of confounders, treatments, and outcomes. - Drawing DAGs to clarify temporal ordering and avoid collider bias. - When estimating a per-protocol effect (the effect of sustained adherence), treating adherence as part of the treatment strategy and using IPCW or LTMLE to adjust for post-baseline confounders of adherence, rather than simply conditioning on adherence status.
Diagnostics and sensitivity analysis. Examine whether any covariates in the adjustment set were measured after time zero. If time-varying confounders are present, compare estimates from baseline-only adjustment with those from IPTW or LTMLE to quantify the magnitude of time-varying confounding bias. As a sensitivity analysis, assess how results change when suspected mediators are removed from or added to the adjustment set.
crumbs: Appendices
26  Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

title: 26  Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them
section: 26.3 Pitfall: Violated Positivity or Limited Overlap
text:
26.3 Pitfall: Violated Positivity or Limited Overlap

26.3.1 Treatment not comparable across covariate strata
Symptoms: - Propensity scores close to 0 or 1 - Large or highly variable IPTW weights - Extreme clever covariate values in TMLE
Why it matters: Positivity violations can lead to instability, high variance, and in finite samples, estimand drift toward extrapolation. But the consequences extend beyond estimator performance. Positivity is an identifiability condition: if certain covariate strata contain patients who would never receive one of the treatment arms, the causal effect is not nonparametrically identified in those strata. No amount of modeling flexibility can recover information that the data do not contain. This is “practical positivity”; a statement about the data-generating process, not merely about the estimator.
RWE example. A study compares warfarin to direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation. Patients aged over 90 with severe renal impairment almost never receive DOACs in the data (probability near zero). The ATE for this subgroup is not identifiable from the observed data. Including these patients inflates weights, destabilizes variance, and shifts the estimate toward extrapolation. The analyst must decide whether to restrict the population, change the estimand, or report the limitation.


26.3.2 Structured overlap diagnostic workflow
Positivity assessment should follow a structured sequence, not a single plot.

Propensity score overlap plot. Plot the distribution of estimated propensity scores by treatment group (e.g., mirrored histograms or density plots). Look for regions where one group has near-zero density.
Weight distribution. Compute stabilized IPTW weights and examine the distribution: maximum weight, ratio of maximum to median, and the proportion of total weight carried by the top 1% of observations. If a small number of observations carry a large share of the total weight, the estimate is fragile.
Effective sample size (ESS). Calculate \(\text{ESS} = (\sum w_i)^2 / \sum w_i^2\) for each treatment group. If the ESS is substantially smaller than the actual sample size (e.g., less than half), the analysis is relying heavily on a small number of reweighted observations.
Sensitivity to truncation. Estimate the treatment effect at several truncation thresholds (e.g., truncating weights at the 95th, 99th, and 99.5th percentiles). If the point estimate shifts substantially with truncation, the result depends on the tails of the weight distribution; a sign that the estimand may not be well-supported.



26.3.3 Decision rule for positivity failures
When diagnostics reveal positivity problems, the analyst faces three options, in order of preference:

Restrict the population to common support. Exclude covariate strata where one treatment arm has near-zero probability. This changes the target population (and therefore the estimand) to one that is identifiable. Report the restriction transparently.
Change the estimand to a support-aware alternative. When static interventions (“set treatment to 1 for everyone”) violate positivity, consider estimands defined by interventions that respect the natural treatment assignment:

Stochastic interventions define a policy that shifts the probability of treatment rather than setting it deterministically. For example, “increase the probability of DOAC initiation by 20% for each patient.” These estimands are identified even when some patients have near-zero probability of one treatment, because the intervention does not force treatment where it would not naturally occur.
Incremental propensity score interventions multiply the odds of treatment by a fixed factor (e.g., doubling the odds of treatment for everyone). These provide a smooth, interpretable contrast that avoids extrapolation.
Modified treatment policies (MTPs) define the intervention as a function of the natural value of treatment. For continuous or time-varying exposures, an MTP might specify “reduce dose by 10% at each time point relative to the dose the patient would naturally receive.” The lmtp package implements longitudinal MTPs with TMLE-based estimation.

Redefining the estimand is not a concession of failure. It is often the correct scientific response to a structural feature of the data-generating process. If the data cannot support the original question, asking a closely related question that the data can answer is more informative than forcing an unstable answer to the original one.
Report that the original estimand is not credibly identifiable. If neither restriction nor estimand modification produces a scientifically meaningful question, state explicitly that the data do not support the intended causal comparison. This is a legitimate and important finding.

Avoid by: - Inspecting propensity score overlap and weight distributions using the workflow above. - Truncating or stabilizing weights when justified, and reporting sensitivity to truncation level. - Restricting to regions of common support when positivity violations are concentrated in identifiable subgroups. - Considering stochastic interventions or modified treatment policies when static interventions are not feasible in the observed data.
Diagnostics and sensitivity analysis. Report the propensity score overlap plot, weight distribution summary statistics (max, ESS, proportion of weight in top 1%), and estimates under at least two truncation thresholds. If restricting the population, report the characteristics of excluded patients and discuss how the restriction changes the target population. Conduct a sensitivity analysis comparing the ATE on the full population with the ATE on the restricted population and, if feasible, with a stochastic intervention estimand.

title: 26  Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them
section: 26.4 Pitfall: Misspecified Outcome or Propensity Models
text:
26.4 Pitfall: Misspecified Outcome or Propensity Models

26.4.1 Relying on simple models in complex settings
Mistake: Assuming linearity, additivity, or simple parametric relationships without support.
Why it matters: Misspecification of nuisance functions can create bias, especially for non-collapsible estimands or when overlap is limited. For IPTW, misspecification of the propensity score model leads to biased weights and biased treatment effect estimates. For g-computation, misspecification of the outcome model directly biases the estimate. TMLE and AIPW offer double robustness; consistency if either the outcome model or the treatment model is correctly specified; but this protection is only asymptotic and depends on regularity conditions. In finite samples with substantial misspecification of both models, even doubly robust estimators can be biased.
RWE example. A study of metformin initiation and HbA1c reduction fits a linear outcome model adjusting for age, BMI, baseline HbA1c, and eGFR as main effects. However, the relationship between baseline HbA1c and the outcome is strongly nonlinear (patients with higher baseline HbA1c have more room for reduction), and there is an interaction between BMI and metformin effect. The linear model misses both features. When the outcome model is used in g-computation, the estimated ATE is biased. When used as the initial estimate in TMLE, the targeting step can partially correct the bias if the propensity score model is well-specified, but the correction is incomplete if the propensity score model is also too rigid.
Avoid by: - Using SuperLearner or other data-adaptive estimation with cross-validation to reduce dependence on a single parametric specification. SuperLearner combines multiple candidate learners (e.g., generalized linear models, random forests, gradient boosting, multivariate adaptive regression splines) using cross-validated risk to select the optimal weighted combination. - Inspecting calibration and predictive performance (for example, cross-validated risk, calibration plots for propensity scores). - Including domain-informed learners alongside flexible algorithms when strong structure is known (e.g., a parametric learner that includes a known interaction, alongside data-adaptive learners that can discover additional structure).
Diagnostics and sensitivity analysis. Report the cross-validated risk of the SuperLearner ensemble and the relative contribution of each candidate learner. For propensity scores, produce calibration plots (predicted probability versus observed treatment proportion in deciles). Compare estimates from a simple parametric model with estimates from SuperLearner-based TMLE. If the two diverge, investigate which model features drive the difference. As a robustness check, vary the SuperLearner library (e.g., add or remove highly flexible learners) and assess stability of the treatment effect estimate.

26  Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

title: 26  Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them
text:
26.5 Pitfall: Blindly Trusting Machine Learning

26.5.1 Flexible models do not guarantee valid inference
Mistake: Assuming that a strong predictive model implies a correct causal estimate.
Why it matters: Machine learning can overfit, extrapolate, or yield poorly calibrated probabilities. Prediction quality alone does not ensure correct estimation of a causal functional. A model that achieves excellent cross-validated AUC for treatment prediction is not necessarily a good propensity score model for causal inference: the model may be well-calibrated on average but poorly calibrated in the tails, precisely where causal estimation is most sensitive. Furthermore, a highly flexible learner fit on the full sample can produce plug-in bias; the nuisance parameter estimate overfits to the sample in a way that biases the final causal estimate, even asymptotically, unless proper sample-splitting is used.


26.5.2 Prediction versus causal estimation
The objective in nuisance estimation is not to maximize prediction accuracy in the population. It is to obtain consistent estimates of nuisance functions (the outcome regression and the treatment/censoring mechanisms) that, when plugged into the causal functional, yield a consistent and efficient estimate of the target parameter. A model that is overfit to the training data will produce nuisance estimates that are too responsive to sample-specific noise. When these are plugged into the TMLE or AIPW estimator, the result inherits that noise, producing standard errors that are too narrow and confidence intervals with incorrect coverage.


26.5.3 Cross-fitting as a guardrail
Cross-fitting (also called sample splitting) partitions the data into K folds. For each fold, nuisance functions are estimated on the remaining K-1 folds and evaluated on the held-out fold. The causal parameter is then estimated using the cross-fitted nuisance predictions. This eliminates the Donsker-class condition that would otherwise be required for consistency when using highly adaptive learners (e.g., random forests, boosted trees, neural networks). Cross-validated TMLE (CV-TMLE) implements this approach and should be preferred whenever the SuperLearner library includes learners that are more complex than parametric regression.


26.5.4 ML leakage checklist
Before fitting any machine learning model for nuisance estimation, verify:

No future information. Features used to predict treatment at time \(t\) must be measured at or before time \(t\). Features used to predict the outcome must not include post-treatment variables unless the estimand explicitly requires them (e.g., mediation).
No post-baseline variables in the propensity score model unless the analysis uses a longitudinal framework (LTMLE) that explicitly models the time-varying treatment mechanism.
No biased feature construction. Derived variables (e.g., “number of prior hospitalizations”) must not inadvertently encode outcome information or use look-ahead data.
No outcome information in the treatment model. The propensity score model must not include the outcome or variables determined after the outcome.



26.5.5 Targeted learning diagnostics
When using TMLE or AIPW, the following diagnostics help assess whether nuisance estimation is adequate for causal inference:
Clever covariate extremes. In TMLE, the clever covariate \(H(A, W) = I(A=1)/g(W) - I(A=0)/(1-g(W))\) drives the targeting step. When propensity scores \(g(W)\) are near 0 or 1, the clever covariate becomes extreme, and the targeting step can produce large, unstable updates. Inspect the distribution of clever covariate values. If a small number of observations have values orders of magnitude larger than the rest, those observations dominate the targeting step and may destabilize the estimate.
Influence curve stability. The efficient influence curve (EIC) provides observation-level contributions to the parameter estimate. Compute the EIC for each observation and inspect the distribution. Observations with EIC values far from the mean (e.g., beyond 5 standard deviations) exert disproportionate influence. Investigate whether these correspond to positivity violations, extreme covariate values, or data errors.
Collaborative targeting. When nuisance estimation is challenging (e.g., rare outcomes, near-violations of positivity), standard TMLE may benefit from collaborative targeted learning approaches that jointly optimize the outcome and treatment models to minimize the variance of the target parameter, rather than optimizing each model independently. This is conceptually similar to choosing the propensity score model that produces the most efficient TMLE, rather than the one that best predicts treatment. When standard diagnostics reveal instability, consider whether collaborative targeting or regularization of the targeting step improves performance.
Avoid by: - Using strict cross-validation and cross-fitting (CV-TMLE) when the learner library includes flexible algorithms. - Checking calibration (especially for propensity scores) and inspecting clever covariate distributions. - Using TMLE or other doubly robust approaches to reduce sensitivity to nuisance estimation errors, subject to regularity conditions. - Applying the ML leakage checklist before model fitting.
Diagnostics and sensitivity analysis. Report cross-validated risk for each nuisance model, propensity score calibration plots, the distribution of clever covariate values, and a summary of influence curve extremes (e.g., number of observations with EIC values beyond \(\pm 3\) or \(\pm 5\) standard deviations of the mean). As a sensitivity analysis, compare estimates from CV-TMLE with estimates from non-cross-fitted TMLE. If they differ substantially, overfitting of nuisance models is likely a concern.

title: 26  Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them
section: 26.6 Pitfall: Ignoring Censoring and Informative Dropout
text:
26.6 Pitfall: Ignoring Censoring and Informative Dropout
Mistake: Treating censoring as non-informative without justification, or ignoring treatment discontinuation and switching when they affect the estimand.
Why it matters: Informative censoring can bias estimates of risk and survival functionals. In pharmacoepidemiology, censoring is rarely truly random. Patients who discontinue treatment, switch to a competitor drug, transfer care, or die from competing causes are typically different from those who remain under observation. If the factors driving censoring also affect the outcome (informative censoring), naive analyses that treat censored observations as non-informative will be biased.
The connection to the estimand is critical. Whether treatment discontinuation or switching constitutes “censoring” or is part of the estimand depends on the causal question. Under a treatment-policy (intention-to-treat analog) estimand, switching is part of the natural course and patients are followed regardless. Under a per-protocol estimand, deviation from the assigned strategy is an intercurrent event that must be handled; either through censoring at deviation and IPCW adjustment, or through a hypothetical estimand that models the counterfactual under sustained adherence.
RWE example. A study compares two antihypertensive regimens for a composite cardiovascular endpoint over five years. Twenty percent of patients in one arm switch to the other within two years, and switching is more common among patients with poorly controlled blood pressure (a predictor of the outcome). Under a per-protocol estimand, naive censoring at switch removes the highest-risk patients from one arm, biasing the estimate toward the null. Under a treatment-policy estimand, all patients are followed regardless of switching, but the estimate reflects the real-world treatment trajectory (including switching) rather than the pharmacological effect of sustained therapy.
Avoid by: - Defining censoring relative to the estimand in the target trial protocol. Distinguish administrative censoring (end of data availability, unrelated to health status) from informative censoring (loss to follow-up, switching, competing events). - Modeling censoring mechanisms (for example, with SuperLearner) and applying IPCW when the censoring mechanism depends on measured covariates. - Using TMLE/LTMLE with explicit censoring nodes in longitudinal settings. LTMLE jointly models the treatment, censoring, and outcome processes at each time point, providing doubly robust estimation under sequential exchangeability. - Specifying how adherence and switching are handled in the estimand. Switching can be part of the treatment strategy (treatment-policy), a censoring event (per-protocol with IPCW), or the basis of a hypothetical estimand (what would have happened if no one switched). The choice determines the analysis method.
Diagnostics and sensitivity analysis. Report the rate and timing of censoring by treatment arm and by key baseline covariates. If IPCW is used, report the distribution of censoring weights and the effective sample size. Compare estimates from (a) naive complete-case analysis, (b) IPCW-adjusted analysis, and (c) LTMLE with censoring nodes. As a sensitivity analysis, assess how the estimate changes under plausible violations of the independent censoring assumption: for example, assume that censored patients have 20%, 50%, or 100% higher event rates than non-censored patients with the same covariates, and bound the resulting bias.
